15\.174 · Linear Movement · Lead, Bring, Take

to lead out, to bring forth to lead or bring out of a structure or area

[MAT 13:52](https://ref.ly/Matt13:52) · [MRK 1:12](https://ref.ly/Matt1:12)1 · [JHN 10:4](https://ref.ly/Luke10:4)1

53\.102 · Religious Activities · Exorcism

to cast out, to make go out, to exorcise to cause a demon to no longer possess or control a person The process of exorcism is expressed in different languages in a number of diverse ways, for example, ‘to make leave,’ ‘to chase away,’ ‘to force to run,’ ‘to destroy,’ ‘to overpower,’ or ‘to kill.’

13\.68 · Be, Become, Exist, Happen · Change of State

to cause to be, to make become to cause a significant change of state by decisive action In some languages, however, it may be extremely difficult to speak of ‘justice prevailing.’ The closest equivalent may be ‘until he causes all people to be treated justly’ or ‘until authorities treat all people in a just manner.’

[MAT 12:20](https://ref.ly/Matt12:20)

33\.396 · Communication · Insult, Slander

**Collocation:** ἐκβάλλω τὸ ὄνομα

to insult, to slander (an idiom, literally: to throw out the name) to insult or slander, with a possible implication of a kind of psychological ostracism

[LUK 6:22](https://ref.ly/Mark6:22)

1 MRK 1:12 · JHN 10:4 It would be possible to understand ἐκβάλλω in [MRK 1:12](https://ref.ly/Matt1:12) as meaning ‘to force out’ (see [15\.44](#15.44)) and thus to translate ‘the Spirit forced him out into the desert,’ but this meaning does not apply to [JHN 10:4](https://ref.ly/Luke10:4), where the meaning of ‘to lead’ would be the appropriate rendering.

## ἔκβασις (id: 001558000000000)

ἔκβασις
=======

ἔκβασις (G1545)

noun · βαίνω

67\.68 · Time · A Point of Time with Reference to Duration of Time: Beginning, End

end (a figurative extension of meaning of ἔκβασις ‘way out of,’ not occurring in the NT) the end point of a duration, but often with the implication of outcome or result For another interpretation of ἔκβασις in [HEB 13:7](https://ref.ly/Phlm13:7), see [89\.39](#89.39).

[HEB 13:7](https://ref.ly/Phlm13:7)

89\.39 · Relations · Result

result, outcome the outcome of an event or state It is also possible to understand ἔκβασις in [HEB 13:7](https://ref.ly/Phlm13:7) as meaning ‘the end’ of one’s life (see [67\.68](#67.68)).

[HEB 13:7](https://ref.ly/Phlm13:7)

21\.16 · Danger, Risk, Safe, Save · Become Safe, Free from Danger

a means of escape, a way of escape a means by which one may escape from some danger or difficulty In the context of [1CO 10:13](https://ref.ly/Rom10:13) πειρασμός may mean either ‘temptation’ or ‘testing.’

[1CO 10:13](https://ref.ly/Rom10:13)

* **Associated Passages:** Hebrews 13:7; 1 Corinthians 10:13

## ἔκγονος (id: 001560000000000)

ἔκγονος
=======

ἔκγονος (G1549) — ἔκγονον

adj · γίνομαι

10\.48 · Kinship Terms · Kinship Relations Involving Successive Generations

grandchild the offspring of one’s children In the one passage in which ἔκγονος occurs in the NT (namely, [1TI 5:4](https://ref.ly/2Thess5:4)), the reference is to adult grandchildren, and in some languages this element in the meaning must be made explicit. In languages in which there is no generic term to include both ‘grandsons’ and ‘granddaughters,’ it may be necessary to translate ἔκγονος in [1TI 5:4](https://ref.ly/2Thess5:4) as ‘grandsons or granddaughters.’

[1TI 5:4](https://ref.ly/2Thess5:4)

* **Associated Passages:** 1 Timothy 5:4


## ἐκδαπανάω (id: 001561000000000)

ἐκδαπανάω
=========

ἐκδαπανάω (G1550) — ἐκδαπανάομαι

δαπανάω

42\.28 · Perform, Do · Do, Perform

to give oneself completely for, to do anything and everything (a figurative extension of meaning of ἐκδαπανάω ‘to spend completely,’ not occurring in the NT) to do anything and everything to the limit of one’s capacity If one wishes to preserve something of the underlying figurative meaning of this part of [2CO 12:15](https://ref.ly/1Cor12:15), one may translate ‘I will gladly spend everything and be completely spent on your behalf.’

[2CO 12:15](https://ref.ly/1Cor12:15)1

1 2CO 12:15 In [2CO 12:15](https://ref.ly/1Cor12:15) it is clear that ἐκδαπανάω is to be understood in a more intensive sense that δαπανάω\[c] ([42\.27](#42.27)).

* **Associated Passages:** 2 Corinthians 12:15


## ἐκδέχομαι (id: 001562000000000)

ἐκδέχομαι
=========

ἐκδέχομαι (G1551)

δέχομαι

85\.60 · Existence in Space · Remain, Stay

to await, to wait for to remain in a place and/or state, with expectancy concerning a future event1 This meaning is also found as a variant reading: ἐκδεχομένων τὴν τοῦ ὕδατος κίνησιν ‘waiting for the movement of the water’ [JHN 5:3](https://ref.ly/Luke5:3) (apparatus).

[JHN 5:3](https://ref.ly/Luke5:3) · [ACT 17:16](https://ref.ly/John17:16) · [1CO 11:33](https://ref.ly/Rom11:33) · [1CO 16:11](https://ref.ly/Rom16:11) · [HEB 11:10](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:10) · [JAS 5:7](https://ref.ly/Heb5:7)

30\.53 · Think · To Think Concerning Future Contingencies

to expect, expectation to expect something to happen, often implying waiting The closest equivalent of ‘to expect’ is usually ‘to think that it will happen that.’

[1CO 16:11](https://ref.ly/Rom16:11) · [HEB 11:10](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:10)

13\.28 · Be, Become, Exist, Happen · State

to remain until, to wait until to continue to remain in a state until an expected event

[HEB 10:13](https://ref.ly/Phlm10:13)

1 There are probably certain subtle distinctions of meaning in this set. The terms containing the stem μεν\- probably focus more upon location, while with the stem δεχ\- the focus may be more upon anticipation, but there is no consistent patterning on the basis of this type of distinction.

* **Associated Passages:** John 5:3; Acts 17:16; 1 Corinthians 11:33; 1 Corinthians 16:11; Hebrews 11:10; James 5:7; Hebrews 10:13

adj · δηλόω

ἐκδημέω
=======

ἐκδημέω (G1553)

δῆμος

85\.21 · Existence in Space · Be in a Place

to be absent, to be away from to be absent from a place where one rightfully or normally belongs

[2CO 5:6](https://ref.ly/1Cor5:6) · [2CO 5:9](https://ref.ly/1Cor5:9)

23\.111 · Physiological Processes and States · Live, Die

## ἐκδίδωμι (id: 001565000000000)

ἐκδίδωμι
========

ἐκδίδωμι (G1554) — ἐκδίδομαι

δίδωμι

57\.177 · Possess, Transfer, Exchange · Hire, Rent Out

to let out, to lease, to rent out to permit the use of property or assets in exchange for remuneration In some languages it may be necessary to specify quite clearly the implications of ‘renting out land,’ for example, ‘he let farmers grow crops on his land in exchange for a part of the harvest.’

[MAT 21:33](https://ref.ly/Matt21:33) · [MAT 21:41](https://ref.ly/Matt21:41) · [MRK 12:1](https://ref.ly/Matt12:1) · [LUK 20:9](https://ref.ly/Mark20:9)

1 It is possible that ἐξηγέομαι\[a], διηγέομαι, and ἐκδιηγέομαι differ somewhat in emphasis or connotation, but it is impossible to determine this from NT usage.

39\.33 · Hostility, Strife · Revenge

to pay back, to revenge, to seek retribution, retribution, seeking retribution to repay harm with harm, on the assumption that the initial harm was unjustified and that retribution is therefore called for

## ἐκδίκησις (id: 001568000000000)

ἐκδίκησις
=========

ἐκδίκησις (G1557)

noun · δικαιόω

56\.35 · Courts and Legal Procedures · Obtain Justice

to give someone justice to give justice to someone who has been wronged

[LUK 18:7](https://ref.ly/Mark18:7)1 · [LUK 18:8](https://ref.ly/Mark18:8) · [ACT 7:24](https://ref.ly/John7:24)

39\.33 · Hostility, Strife · Revenge

to pay back, to revenge, to seek retribution, retribution, seeking retribution to repay harm with harm, on the assumption that the initial harm was unjustified and that retribution is therefore called for

[LUK 21:22](https://ref.ly/Mark21:22) · [ACT 7:24](https://ref.ly/John7:24) · [ROM 12:19](https://ref.ly/Acts12:19) · [2CO 7:11](https://ref.ly/1Cor7:11) · [2TH 1:8](https://ref.ly/1Thess1:8) · [HEB 10:30](https://ref.ly/Phlm10:30)

38\.8 · Punish, Reward · Punish

to punish, to cause to suffer, punishment to punish, on the basis of what is rightly deserved

[2CO 7:11](https://ref.ly/1Cor7:11) · [2TH 1:8](https://ref.ly/1Thess1:8) · [HEB 10:30](https://ref.ly/Phlm10:30) · [1PE 2:14](https://ref.ly/Jas2:14)

1 LUK 18:7 It is difficult to determine whether ἐκδίκησις in [LUK 18:7](https://ref.ly/Mark18:7) should be interpreted as a state of justice or the activity of giving justice. One might assume that the activity is represented by the verb ποιέω and that ἐκδίκησις then denotes the resulting state, but it is also possible to understand ποιέω as a kind of empty verb, which simply marks a case relationship between the agent (ὁ θεός) and the primary activity in ἐκδίκησις.

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 18:7; Luke 18:8; Acts 7:24; Luke 21:22; Romans 12:19; 2 Corinthians 7:11; 2 Thessalonians 1:8; Hebrews 10:30; 1 Peter 2:14

=======

adj · δικαιόω

punisher (derivative of ἐκδικέω\[c] ‘to punish,’ [38\.8](#38.8)) a person who punishes

## ἐκδιώκω (id: 001570000000000)

ἐκδιώκω
=======

ἐκδιώκω (G1559)

διώκω

15\.159 · Linear Movement · Pursue, Follow

to pursue and drive out to pursue to the point of driving out ἐκδιώκω in [1TH 2:15](https://ref.ly/Col2:15) may also be understood in the sense of ‘to persecute’ (see [39\.45](#39.45)).

[1TH 2:15](https://ref.ly/Col2:15)2

39\.45 · Hostility, Strife · Persecution

to persecute, to harass, persecution to systematically organize a program to oppress and harass people1 In a number of languages the equivalent of ‘to persecute’ is simply ‘to cause to suffer,’ but persecution is also expressed in terms of ‘to be mean to’ or ‘to threaten’ or ‘to chase from place to place.’\|For another interpretation of ἐκδιώκω in [1TH 2:15](https://ref.ly/Col2:15), see [15\.159](#15.159).

[1TH 2:15](https://ref.ly/Col2:15)2

1 Some persons have contended that ἐκδιώκω\[b] is an intensive of διώκω\[c] with the resulting meaning of ‘to persecute harshly.’ However, general Greek usage and particularly NT contexts do not seem to bear out any such consistent distinctions in meaning. 2 1TH 2:15 In a parallel type of context, [LUK 11:49](https://ref.ly/Mark11:49) reads ἐξ αὐτῶν ἀποκτενοῦσιν καὶ διώξουσιν ‘they will kill some and persecute others,’ for which some manuscripts read ἐκδιώξουσιν in place of διώξουσιν.

* **Associated Passages:** 1 Thessalonians 2:15

83\.2 · Spacial Positions · Here, There

there, at that place a position relatively far from the speaker, writer, or viewpoint person2

[LUK 19:4](https://ref.ly/Mark19:4) · [HEB 11:15](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:15)

1 In some contexts, for example, [MRK 16:10](https://ref.ly/Matt16:10), the implication of absence in a discourse setting becomes relatively weak. In a number of contexts both οὗτος ([92\.29](#92.29)) and ἐκεῖνος ([92\.30](#92.30)) may be translated by personal pronouns such as he, she, it, they. 2 As in the case of ὧδε\[a] and ἐνθάδε ([83\.1](#83.1)), the semantic component of movement is not necessarily found in ἐκεῖ, ἐκεῖσε, and ἐκείνης.

## ἐκεῖσε (id: 001577000000000)

ἐκεῖσε
======

ἐκεῖσε (G1566)

adv · ἐκεῖ

83\.2 · Spacial Positions · Here, There

there, at that place a position relatively far from the speaker, writer, or viewpoint person1

1 As in the case of ὧδε\[a] and ἐνθάδε ([83\.1](#83.1)), the semantic component of movement is not necessarily found in ἐκεῖ, ἐκεῖσε, and ἐκείνης.

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 21:3; Acts 22:5

## ἐκζήτησις (id: 001579000000000)

ἐκζήτησις
=========

ἐκζήτησις (G2214a)

noun · ζητέω

31\.33 · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Suppose, Think Possible

speculation, worthless speculation, imaginings to form ideas which are unrelated to reality, normally with a negative connotation It is also possible to understand ἐκζήτησις in [1TI 1:4](https://ref.ly/2Thess1:4) as meaning ‘controversies’ or ‘arguments’ (see [33\.442](#33.442)).

[1TI 1:4](https://ref.ly/2Thess1:4)

33\.442 · Communication · Dispute, Debate

idle dispute, meaningless speculation a dispute involving empty speculation For another interpretation of ἐκζήτησις in [1TI 1:4](https://ref.ly/2Thess1:4), see [31\.33](#31.33).

[1TI 1:4](https://ref.ly/2Thess1:4)

* **Associated Passages:** 1 Timothy 1:4

καίω

25\.16 · Attitudes and Emotions · Desire Strongly

**Collocation:** ἐκκαίομαι ἐν τῇ ὀρέξει

to be inflamed with passion, to have a strong lust for, to be inflamed with lust (an idiom, literally: to burn with intense desire) to have a strong, intense desire for something In some languages the equivalent idiom is ‘to boil with desire,’ ‘to feel hot in the genitals,’ or ‘to prefer to die rather than to do.’

## ἐκκλείω (id: 001588000000000)

ἐκκλείω
=======

ἐκκλείω (G1576)

κλείω

13\.143 · Be, Become, Exist, Happen · Happen

to eliminate, to not allow, to exclude to cause something to be excluded or not allowed It is also possible to understand ἐκκλείω in [ROM 3:27](https://ref.ly/Acts3:27) as meaning that there is no basis for boasting; in other words, there is nothing that one can boast about.

[ROM 3:27](https://ref.ly/Acts3:27)

34\.36 · Association · Belong To, Be Included in the Membership of, Be Excluded From

to exclude, to separate, to get rid of to exclude or remove someone from an association It may also be possible to translate this expression in [GAL 4:17](https://ref.ly/2Cor4:17) as ‘they want you to no longer belong (to me)’ or ‘they want you to exclude (me) from your company.’

[GAL 4:17](https://ref.ly/2Cor4:17)

* **Associated Passages:** Romans 3:27; Galatians 4:17


## ἐκκλησία (id: 001589000000000)

ἐκκλησία
========

ἐκκλησία (G1577)

noun · καλέω

11\.32 · Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes · Socio\-Religious

congregation, church a congregation of Christians, implying interacting membership Though some persons have tried to see in the term ἐκκλησία a more or less literal meaning of ‘called\-out ones,’ this type of etymologizing is not warranted either by the meaning of ἐκκλησία in NT times or even by its earlier usage. The term ἐκκλησία was in common usage for several hundred years before the Christian era and was used to refer to an assembly of persons constituted by well\-defined membership. In general Greek usage it was normally a socio\-political entity based upon citizenship in a city\-state (see ἐκκλησία\[c], [11\.78](#11.78)) and in this sense is parallel to δῆμος ([11\.78](#11.78)). For the NT, however, it is important to understand the meaning of ἐκκλησία\[a] as ‘an assembly of God’s people.’\|In the rendering of ἐκκλησία\[a] a translator must beware of using a term which refers primarily to a building rather than to a congregation of believers. In many contexts ἐκκλησία\[a] may be readily rendered as ‘gathering of believers’ or ‘group of those who trust in Christ.’ Sometimes, as in [1CO 1:2](https://ref.ly/Rom1:2), it is possible to translate ‘Paul writes to the believers in Christ who live in Corinth.’ Such a translation does, however, omit a significant element in the term ἐκκλησία\[a], in that the sense of corporate unity is not specified.

church the totality of congregations of Christians

11\.78 · Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes · Socio\-Political

assembly, gathering a group of citizens assembled for socio\-political activities It is possible that in ἐκκλησία\[c] there is somewhat more focus upon the people being together as a legal assembly, while in the case of δῆμος the emphasis is merely upon a meeting of citizens. But in the NT one cannot distinguish clearly between the meanings of these two words.\|ἐκκλησία\[c] and δῆμος may be rendered in some languages as ‘a meeting of the people who belonged to that place’ or ‘... who were inhabitants of that town’ or ‘... whose homes were in that town.’

[ACT 7:38](https://ref.ly/John7:38) · [ACT 19:32](https://ref.ly/John19:32) · [ACT 19:39](https://ref.ly/John19:39) · [ACT 19:40](https://ref.ly/John19:40) · [1CO 11:18](https://ref.ly/Rom11:18) · [HEB 2:12](https://ref.ly/Phlm2:12) · [HEB 12:23](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:23)

ἐκλέγω
======

λέγω

30\.86 · Think · To Choose, To Select, To Prefer

to choose, to select, to prefer to make a choice of one or more possible alternatives In a number of languages the choice of terms or expressions for ‘choosing’ or ‘selecting’ often depends upon either (1\) what is chosen or selected or (2\) the purpose for such a choice, for example, personal pleasure, rational evaluation, or outright prejudice.

[ACT 1:2](https://ref.ly/John1:2) · [ACT 1:24](https://ref.ly/John1:24) · [1CO 1:27](https://ref.ly/Rom1:27) · [1CO 1:27](https://ref.ly/Rom1:27) · [1CO 1:28](https://ref.ly/Rom1:28) · [JAS 2:5](https://ref.ly/Heb2:5)

30\.92 · Think · To Choose, To Select, To Prefer

to choose, choice to make a special choice based upon significant preference, often implying a strongly favorable attitude toward what is chosen

1 LUK 9:35 The occurrence of ἐκλελεγμένος in [LUK 9:35](https://ref.ly/Mark9:35) is paralleled by ἀγαπητός or ἀγαπητός, ἐν ᾧ εὐδόκησα in Mark and Matthew, respectively. 2 ACT 13:17 In [ACT 13:17](https://ref.ly/John13:17) ἐκλέγομαι\[b] parallels closely the meaning of χάρις\[b] ([57\.103](#57.103)) in referring to God’s special favor or grace.

1 It would be possible to interpret ἐκλείπω\[a] as a change in a state of existence, and hence it could be treated as a negation of existence (Domain 13\), but one could also analyze the meaning of ἐκλείπω\[a] as a negation of quantity and thus treat it in Domain 59\.

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 16:9; Luke 22:32; Luke 23:45; Hebrews 1:12

## ἑκούσιος (id: 001607000000000)

ἑκούσιος
========

ἑκούσιος (G1595)

adj · ἑκών

25\.65 · Attitudes and Emotions · Be Willing

willing, willingly, of one’s own free will pertaining to being willing to do something without being forced or pressured In a number of languages ‘willingness’ to perform some action is expressed negatively, for example, ‘not having to’ or ‘not being forced to’ or ‘not being told one must.’ A positive expression of ‘willingness’ may be indicated idiomatically by a phrase such as ‘my heart approves.’

[PHM 1:14](https://ref.ly/Titus1:14)

* **Associated Passages:** Philemon 1:14

## ἔκπαλαι (id: 001609000000000)

ἔκπαλαι
=======

ἔκπαλαι (G1597)

adv · παλαιόω

67\.24 · Time · A Point of Time with Reference to Other Points of Time: Before, Long Ago, Now, At the Same Time, When, About, After

long ago, of long ago pertaining to a point of time preceding another point of time, with an interval of considerable length1 References to a point of time preceding another point of time may express a relationship to an event in the discourse or to the time of the discourse itself. In [HEB 1:1](https://ref.ly/Phlm1:1) πάλαι\[a] marks a point of time long before the actual writing of this Letter to the Hebrews. The same is true of ἔκπαλαι in [2PE 3:5](https://ref.ly/1Pet3:5). In some languages it is necessary to indicate clearly whether the interval is (1\) between a prior time and the event mentioned in the discourse or (2\) between a prior time and the discourse itself. For example, in [HEB 1:1](https://ref.ly/Phlm1:1) it may be necessary to translate ‘long ago before now God spoke to our ancestors through the prophets’ or ‘long ago before now that I am writing ...’

[2PE 3:5](https://ref.ly/1Pet3:5)

67\.132 · Time · Duration of Time with Reference to Some Point of Time: Until, Delay, Still, From

since a long time, for a long time an extensive period of time from a point in the past

[2PE 2:3](https://ref.ly/1Pet2:3)

1 It is possible that ἔκπαλαι\[a] is somewhat more emphatic than πάλαι\[a], but this cannot be determined from existing contexts.

* **Associated Passages:** 2 Peter 3:5; 2 Peter 2:3


## ἐκπειράζω (id: 001610000000000)

ἐκπειράζω
=========

ἐκπειράζω (G1598)

πειράζω

27\.46 · Learn · Try To Learn

to test, to examine, to put to the test, examination, testing to try to learn the nature or character of someone or something by submitting such to thorough and extensive testing It is also possible to understand πειράζω and ἐκπειράζω in [MAT 16:1](https://ref.ly/Matt16:1) and [LUK 10:25](https://ref.ly/Mark10:25) as meaning ‘to try to trap’ (see [27\.31](#27.31)). See also [88\.308](#88.308).

[LUK 10:25](https://ref.ly/Mark10:25) · [LUK 4:12](https://ref.ly/Mark4:12) · [1CO 10:9](https://ref.ly/Rom10:9)

27\.31 · Learn · Learn Something Against Someone

to try to trap, to attempt to catch in a mistake to obtain information to be used against a person by trying to cause someone to make a mistake It is also possible to understand πειράζω and ἐκπειράζω in [MAT 16:1](https://ref.ly/Matt16:1) and [LUK 10:25](https://ref.ly/Mark10:25) as ‘the process of testing or examining’ (see [27\.46](#27.46)). See also [88\.308](#88.308).

[LUK 10:25](https://ref.ly/Mark10:25)

88\.308 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Sin, Wrongdoing, Guilt

to tempt, to trap, to lead into temptation, temptation to endeavor or attempt to cause someone to sin It is also possible to understand ἐκπειράζω in [LUK 10:25](https://ref.ly/Mark10:25) as merely a process of testing (see [27\.46](#27.46)) or of trying to trap (see [27\.31](#27.31)).

[MAT 4:7](https://ref.ly/Matt4:7) · [LUK 10:25](https://ref.ly/Mark10:25)

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 10:25; Luke 4:12; 1 Corinthians 10:9; Matthew 4:7

## ἐκπετάννυμι (id: 001613000000000)

===========

πετάννυμι · πέτομαι

16\.19 · Non\-Linear Movement

to stretch out, to extend, to reach out to cause an object to extend in space (for example, by becoming straight, unfolded, or uncoiled)1

[ROM 10:21](https://ref.ly/Acts10:21)

1 With ἐκτείνω and ἐκπετάννυμι there is a type of movement in space, but only a movement of an extremity, not of the base from which the extension takes place. There is a sense in which the occurrence of hand with ἐκτείνω and ἐκπετάννυμι involves the motion of a body part (see Domain 18 Attachment), but since the same terms may be used not only of body parts but of any object which may be extended, it seems more satisfactory to classify all of these related meanings together.

34\.26 · Association · Join, Begin To Associate

to fall away, to forsake, to turn away to abandon a former relationship or association, or to dissociate (a type of reversal of beginning to associate)1 Note, however, that the underlying structure of the expression τῆς χάριτος ἐξεπέσατε really involves ‘turning away from God who has shown grace.’ For another interpretation of ἐκπίπτω in [GAL 5:4](https://ref.ly/2Cor5:4), see [90\.72](#90.72).

## ἐκπορεύομαι (id: 001621000000000)

ἐκπορεύομαι
===========

ἐκπορεύομαι (G1607)

πορεύομαι

15\.40 · Linear Movement · Leave, Depart, Flee, Escape, Send

to go out of, to depart out of, to leave from within to move out of an enclosed or well\-defined two or three\-dimensional area In some contexts one may interpret occurrences of ἐκπορεύομαι as being used figuratively or as part of an idiomatic phrase, but ἐκπορεύομαι also implies some type of movement out from a defined or enclosed area. Note, for example, the occurrences of ἐκπορεύομαι in the following contexts: τὸ ἐκπορευόμενον ἐκ τοῦ στόματος τοῦτο κοινοῖ τὸν ἄνθρωπον ‘that which comes from a man’s mouth causes him to be defiled’ [MAT 15:11](https://ref.ly/Matt15:11); ἐξεπορεύετο ἦχος περὶ αὐτοῦ εἰς πάντα τόπον τῆς περιχώρου ‘the report about him went out into all parts of the surrounding region’ [LUK 4:37](https://ref.ly/Mark4:37); τά τε πνεύματα τὰ πονηρὰ ἐκπορεύεσθαι ‘the evil spirits would go out of them’ [ACT 19:12](https://ref.ly/John19:12). In a number of languages, however, it may be necessary to translate the meaning of ἐκπορεύομαι in quite different ways. For example, in [MAT 15:11](https://ref.ly/Matt15:11) it may be necessary to translate ‘that which a man says causes him to be defiled,’ and in [LUK 4:37](https://ref.ly/Mark4:37) it may be necessary to translate ‘more and more people in all parts of the surrounding region heard about Jesus,’ and for [ACT 19:12](https://ref.ly/John19:12) it may be necessary to say ‘the evil spirits would no longer ride them’ or ‘... control them.’

[MAT 3:5](https://ref.ly/Matt3:5) · [MAT 4:4](https://ref.ly/Matt4:4) · [MAT 15:11](https://ref.ly/Matt15:11) · [MAT 15:18](https://ref.ly/Matt15:18) · [MAT 20:29](https://ref.ly/Matt20:29) · [MRK 1:5](https://ref.ly/Matt1:5) · [MRK 6:11](https://ref.ly/Matt6:11) · [MRK 7:15](https://ref.ly/Matt7:15) · [MRK 7:19](https://ref.ly/Matt7:19) · [MRK 7:20](https://ref.ly/Matt7:20) · [MRK 7:21](https://ref.ly/Matt7:21) · [MRK 7:23](https://ref.ly/Matt7:23) · [MRK 10:17](https://ref.ly/Matt10:17) · [MRK 10:46](https://ref.ly/Matt10:46) · [MRK 11:19](https://ref.ly/Matt11:19) · [MRK 13:1](https://ref.ly/Matt13:1) · [LUK 3:7](https://ref.ly/Mark3:7) · [LUK 4:22](https://ref.ly/Mark4:22) · [LUK 4:37](https://ref.ly/Mark4:37) · [JHN 5:29](https://ref.ly/Luke5:29) · [JHN 15:26](https://ref.ly/Luke15:26) · [ACT 19:12](https://ref.ly/John19:12) · [ACT 25:4](https://ref.ly/John25:4) · [EPH 4:29](https://ref.ly/Gal4:29) · [REV 1:16](https://ref.ly/Jude1:16) · [REV 4:5](https://ref.ly/Jude4:5) · [REV 9:17](https://ref.ly/Jude9:17) · [REV 9:18](https://ref.ly/Jude9:18) · [REV 11:5](https://ref.ly/Jude11:5) · [REV 16:14](https://ref.ly/Jude16:14) · [REV 19:15](https://ref.ly/Jude19:15) · [REV 22:1](https://ref.ly/Jude22:1)

41\.24 · Behavior and Related States · Behavior, Conduct

**Collocation:** εἰσπορεύομαι καὶ ἐκπορεύομαι

to live with, to live among (idiom, literally: to go in and to go out) to live or to conduct oneself in relationship to some community or group The semantic focus of these idioms, especially in [ACT 1:21](https://ref.ly/John1:21) and [ACT 9:28](https://ref.ly/John9:28), appears to be the manner in which a person conducts himself in relationship to others, but it is possible to imply a certain amount of movement. For example, in [ACT 1:21](https://ref.ly/John1:21) it is possible to translate ‘during the whole time the Lord Jesus travelled about with us,’ and in [ACT 9:28](https://ref.ly/John9:28) one could translate ‘Paul stayed with them and went about in Jerusalem.’

[ACT 9:28](https://ref.ly/John9:28)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 3:5; Matthew 4:4; Matthew 15:11; Matthew 15:18; Matthew 20:29; Mark 1:5; Mark 6:11; Mark 7:15; Mark 7:19; Mark 7:20; Mark 7:21; Mark 7:23; Mark 10:17; Mark 10:46; Mark 11:19; Mark 13:1; Luke 3:7; Luke 4:22; Luke 4:37; John 5:29; John 15:26; Acts 19:12; Acts 25:4; Ephesians 4:29; Revelation 1:16; Revelation 4:5; Revelation 9:17; Revelation 9:18; Revelation 11:5; Revelation 16:14; Revelation 19:15; Revelation 22:1; Acts 9:28

noun · ἵστημι

25\.217 · Attitudes and Emotions · Surprise, Astonish

amazement, astonishment a state of intense amazement, to the point of being beside oneself with astonishment In many languages it is simply impossible to speak of ‘being beside oneself with astonishment’ or ‘having astonishment seize someone.’ Though such expressions may seem quite appropriate in English, literal renderings of such statements may be almost totally meaningless in other languages. In [MRK 5:42](https://ref.ly/Matt5:42), for example, one may readily translate ‘they were extremely astonished’ or in [LUK 5:26](https://ref.ly/Mark5:26), ‘they were all very much astonished.’\|In a number of languages, of course, astonishment is expressed in an idiomatic manner, for example, ‘they had to look three times’ or ‘they looked but they couldn’t believe.’

## ἐκτινάσσω (id: 001634000000000)

ἐκτινάσσω
=========

τινάσσω

16\.8 · Non\-Linear Movement

to shake off, to shake out, to shake from to shake something out or off, in order to get rid of an object or a substance In a number of languages quite different terms for shaking will be required for the contexts of [ACT 28:5](https://ref.ly/John28:5) and [LUK 9:5](https://ref.ly/Mark9:5), since in the first instance it is an animate being which clings, and in the second instance it is simply a substance that adheres.\|In a number of languages, however, the symbolic meaning of ‘shaking dust off one’s feet’ is not at all clear, so that it may be necessary to add a type of classifier to indicate that such an action symbolizes the breaking off of association. It is for this reason that the expression ἐκτιναξάμενος τὰ ἱμάτια in [ACT 18:6](https://ref.ly/John18:6) is often translated as ‘he protested by shaking the dust from his clothes.’

## ἔκτρωμα (id: 001639000000000)

ἔκτρωμα
=======

ἔκτρωμα (G1626)

noun · τιτρώσκω · τραυματίζω

23\.55 · Physiological Processes and States · Birth, Procreation

untimely birth an untimely or premature birth There is a certain fundamental problem involved in the rendering of ἔκτρωμα in [1CO 15:8](https://ref.ly/Rom15:8). Here Paul refers to himself, but the event in question is the appearance of Jesus to Paul, evidently on the road to Damascus. The reference, therefore, would seem to be his being born as a Christian. This spiritual birth, however, would appear to be rather late in the process rather than premature. It is for this reason that it may be wise to translate ἔκτρωμα in some instances as ‘untimely birth’ or ‘born at the wrong time,’ rather than indicating that he was ‘born too soon.’

[1CO 15:8](https://ref.ly/Rom15:8)

* **Associated Passages:** 1 Corinthians 15:8

=======

to flee out of, to flee from, to escape to flee from or out of

[ACT 16:27](https://ref.ly/John16:27) · [ACT 19:16](https://ref.ly/John19:16)

21\.14 · Danger, Risk, Safe, Save · Become Safe, Free from Danger

to escape, to avoid to become safe from danger by avoiding or escaping1 The meaning of this part of [ROM 2:3](https://ref.ly/Acts2:3) is ‘to become free from the danger of being judged by God.’ ἵνα κατισχύσητε ἐκφυγεῖν ταῦτα πάντα τὰ μέλλοντα γίνεσθαι ‘in order that you may be able to escape all of these things that are going to happen’ [LUK 21:36](https://ref.ly/Mark21:36). In [ROM 2:3](https://ref.ly/Acts2:3) and [LUK 21:36](https://ref.ly/Mark21:36) ἐκφεύγω should not be understood in the literal sense of running away from (see [15\.63](#15.63)). The meaning is simply not to become involved in such danger.

[LUK 21:36](https://ref.ly/Mark21:36) · [ROM 2:3](https://ref.ly/Acts2:3) · [2CO 11:33](https://ref.ly/1Cor11:33) · [1TH 5:3](https://ref.ly/Col5:3) · [HEB 2:3](https://ref.ly/Phlm2:3) · [HEB 12:25](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:25)

1 It is possible that ἐκφεύγω\[b] and ἀποφεύγω differ somewhat in meaning from φεύγω\[b] and from each other, but it is not possible to determine from existing contexts what the distinctive features might be, except perhaps to indicate that ἐκφεύγω\[b] and ἀποφεύγω may be connotatively somewhat more emphatic than φεύγω\[b]. Note, also, that in this series of terms meaning to escape, there is no special indication of movement, but simply the fact of not having to experience some particular difficulty or danger.

to die as a sacrifice, sacrificial death (an idiom, literally: the blood pours out) to die, with the implication of a sacrificial purpose The relations between the various elements in this clause in [MAT 26:28](https://ref.ly/Matt26:28) must in some instances be made somewhat more specific, for example, ‘by my dying, I am like a sacrifice for many for the purpose of their sins being forgiven.’ See also [23\.107](#23.107).

In classical Greek ῥέω and related derivatives were employed more in poetic discourse.

For example, one term may refer to death primarily as the result of old age, while another term may refer to death caused by sickness, and still other terms may be used for death caused by violence. ## ἐλαία (id: 001649000000000)

ἐλαία
=====

ἐλαία (G1636)

noun · ἔλαιον · ἐλαιών · καλλιέλαιος

3\.9 · Plants · Trees

olive tree tree reaching up to 10 m. high, but usually pruned to around 5 m.; leaves are grayish green above, and whitish underneath; fruit of the olive is about 2 cm. long and a bit more than a cm thick with a hard stone inside and a soft skin that covers the oily flesh; fruit is used for food and oil for cooking, for lamps, for rubbing on the body, for medicine, and in religion (anointing) As in the case of other terms for trees which may not be identified by specific receptor\-language terms, one can employ a generic expression followed by a borrowed term, for example, ‘a tree producing fruit called olives.’ In the context of [MAT 21:1](https://ref.ly/Matt21:1) and similar passages it is important that ‘Mount of Olives’ not be rendered in such a way as to suggest that it was a mountain consisting of olives or even a mountain consisting of olive trees. A more appropriate expression in some languages is ‘a mountain known for its olive trees’ or ‘a mountain on which many olive trees grow.’ In a number of languages, however, it is important to employ a term such as ‘hill’ rather than ‘mountain,’ since the Mount of Olives near Jerusalem would be regarded in most receptor languages as a ‘hill’ not a ‘mountain.’

[MAT 21:1](https://ref.ly/Matt21:1) · [MAT 24:3](https://ref.ly/Matt24:3) · [MAT 26:30](https://ref.ly/Matt26:30) · [MRK 11:1](https://ref.ly/Matt11:1) · [MRK 13:3](https://ref.ly/Matt13:3) · [MRK 14:26](https://ref.ly/Matt14:26) · [LUK 19:29](https://ref.ly/Mark19:29) · [LUK 19:37](https://ref.ly/Mark19:37) · [LUK 21:37](https://ref.ly/Mark21:37) · [LUK 22:39](https://ref.ly/Mark22:39) · [JHN 8:1](https://ref.ly/Luke8:1) · [ROM 11:17](https://ref.ly/Acts11:17) · [ROM 11:24](https://ref.ly/Acts11:24) · [REV 11:4](https://ref.ly/Jude11:4)

93\.616 · Names of Persons and Places · Places

**Collocation:** Ἐλαῖαι · Ὄρος τῶν Ἐλαιῶν

Mount of Olives a ridge to the east of Jerusalem, with the Kidron Valley in between

[MAT 21:1](https://ref.ly/Matt21:1) · [MAT 24:3](https://ref.ly/Matt24:3) · [MAT 26:30](https://ref.ly/Matt26:30) · [MRK 11:1](https://ref.ly/Matt11:1) · [MRK 13:3](https://ref.ly/Matt13:3) · [MRK 14:26](https://ref.ly/Matt14:26) · [LUK 19:29](https://ref.ly/Mark19:29) · [LUK 19:37](https://ref.ly/Mark19:37) · [LUK 21:37](https://ref.ly/Mark21:37) · [LUK 22:39](https://ref.ly/Mark22:39) · [JHN 8:1](https://ref.ly/Luke8:1)

3\.39 · Plants · Fruit Parts of Plants

olive fruit of the olive tree; about 2 cm. long and a bit more than a cm thick with a hard stone inside and a soft skin that covers the oily flesh; used for food and oil for cooking, for lamps, for rubbing on the body, for medicine, and in religion (anointing) Since in some languages a term for ‘olive oil’ (ἔλαιον, [6\.202](#6.202)) is better known than the fruit itself, one may employ a descriptive phrase such as ‘the fruit of olive oil’ or ‘the fruit that produces olive oil.’ In certain instances olives are actually known as ‘the cooking\-oil fruit.’

[JAS 3:12](https://ref.ly/Heb3:12)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 21:1; Matthew 24:3; Matthew 26:30; Mark 11:1; Mark 13:3; Mark 14:26; Luke 19:29; Luke 19:37; Luke 21:37; Luke 22:39; John 8:1; Romans 11:17; Romans 11:24; Revelation 11:4; James 3:12


## ἔλαιον (id: 001650000000000)

ἔλαιον
======

ἔλαιον (G1637)

noun · ἐλαία

6\.202 · Artifacts · Plant Products

olive oil oil extracted from the fruit of olive trees, used as food, medicine, for burning in lamps, and as perfume when mixed with sweet\-smelling substances1 See discussion of translational problems in [6\.197](#6.197).

[MAT 25:3](https://ref.ly/Matt25:3) · [MAT 25:4](https://ref.ly/Matt25:4) · [MAT 25:8](https://ref.ly/Matt25:8) · [MRK 6:13](https://ref.ly/Matt6:13) · [LUK 7:46](https://ref.ly/Mark7:46) · [LUK 10:34](https://ref.ly/Mark10:34) · [LUK 16:6](https://ref.ly/Mark16:6) · [HEB 1:9](https://ref.ly/Phlm1:9) · [JAS 5:14](https://ref.ly/Heb5:14) · [REV 6:6](https://ref.ly/Jude6:6) · [REV 18:13](https://ref.ly/Jude18:13)

1 Though the various terms for wine and olive oil might very well be treated simply in the category of Food (Domain 5\), they obviously served other functions as well, for wine was used medicinally as an antiseptic and olive oil was used not only for food but also for illumination in lamps. Since both the production of wine and olive oil involved relatively elaborate processes, it therefore seems best to classify them as artifacts rather than subsume them under various functional categories.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 25:3; Matthew 25:4; Matthew 25:8; Mark 6:13; Luke 7:46; Luke 10:34; Luke 16:6; Hebrews 1:9; James 5:14; Revelation 6:6; Revelation 18:13

======

noun · ἐλαία

3\.12 · Plants · Trees

olive grove, olive orchard (derivative of ἐλαία\[a] ‘olive tree,’ [3\.9](#3.9)) a number of olive trees planted in a garden or grove In [ACT 1:12](https://ref.ly/John1:12), it may be well to employ the more common designation for ‘the hill called The Olive Grove,’ namely, ‘Hill of Olives’ or ‘Mount of Olives’ (see [3\.9](#3.9)).

[ACT 1:12](https://ref.ly/John1:12)

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 1:12

## ἐλαύνω (id: 001656000000000)

ἐλαύνω
======

ἐλαύνω (G1643)

ἀπελαύνω

15\.161 · Linear Movement · Drive Along, Carry Along

to drive along, to carry along to cause an object to move by means of a strong force or vigorous action In [LUK 8:29](https://ref.ly/Mark8:29) ἐλαύνω occurs in the statement ἠλαύνετο ὑπὸ τοῦ δαιμονίου εἰς τὰς ἐρήμους ‘he was driven into the desert by the demon.’ In this context it would appear that the demon was regarded as a type of force, parallel perhaps to wind.

[MRK 6:48](https://ref.ly/Matt6:48)1 · [LUK 8:29](https://ref.ly/Mark8:29) · [JHN 6:19](https://ref.ly/Luke6:19) · [JAS 3:4](https://ref.ly/Heb3:4) · [2PE 2:17](https://ref.ly/1Pet2:17)

1 MRK 6:48 In [MRK 6:48](https://ref.ly/Matt6:48) the specific reference of ἐλαύνω is to rowing, though the meaning of ἐλαύνω in its more general sense involves simply moving an object by means of force.

* **Associated Passages:** Mark 6:48; Luke 8:29; John 6:19; James 3:4; 2 Peter 2:17


## ἐλαφρία (id: 001657000000000)

ἐλαφρία
=======

ἐλαφρία (G1644)

noun · ἐλαφρός

88\.99 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Sensible Behavior, Senseless Behavior

fickleness behavior characterized by caprice and instability In some instances ἐλαφρία may be rendered in [2CO 1:17](https://ref.ly/1Cor1:17) as ‘not thinking about what I was doing’ or ‘not keeping the same thoughts about what I was doing.’

[2CO 1:17](https://ref.ly/1Cor1:17)

* **Associated Passages:** 2 Corinthians 1:17


## ἐλαφρός (id: 001658000000000)

ἐλαφρός
=======

ἐλαφρός (G1645)

adj · ἐλαφρία

86\.2 · Weight · Heavy, Light

light, not heavy pertaining to being relatively light in weight Though in [MAT 11:30](https://ref.ly/Matt11:30) ἐλαφρός can be understood in a literal sense, the entire statement in this verse is, of course, figurative. Therefore, one can also interpret ἐλαφρός in [MAT 11:30](https://ref.ly/Matt11:30) as figurative (see [22\.38](#22.38)). Note, however, that the phrase ὁ ... ζυγός μου does not refer to something which Christ carries but to something which he places upon his followers. The same is true of the phrase τὸ φορτίον μου ‘my burden,’ which is the burden he gives to his disciples or servants.

[MAT 11:30](https://ref.ly/Matt11:30) · [2CO 4:17](https://ref.ly/1Cor4:17)

78\.10 · Degree · Much, Little \[Positive\-Negative Degree]

limited, light, not intense a strictly limited point on the lower part of a scale of extent

[2CO 4:17](https://ref.ly/1Cor4:17)

22\.38 · Trouble, Hardship, Relief, Favorable Circumstances · Easy, Light

light, easy pertaining to that which is easy to bear or endure It is possible to render ἐλαφρός in [MAT 11:30](https://ref.ly/Matt11:30) as ‘light,’ in the sense of ‘light in weight’ (see [86\.2](#86.2)). However, if ‘burden’ in [MAT 11:30](https://ref.ly/Matt11:30) is understood figuratively, ἐλαφρός can generally also be understood in a figurative sense.

[MAT 11:30](https://ref.ly/Matt11:30)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 11:30; 2 Corinthians 4:17

=========

adj · ἐλαττόω

small, little a small size, relative to the norm for the class of objects in question Note that in the case of a Greek superlative such as ἐλάχιστος, the meaning is not ‘the smallest’ but simply ‘very small.’ This is typical of many uses of the superlative in Greek.

adj

2\.44 · Natural Substances · Precious and Semiprecious Stones and Substances

consisting of ivory, made of ivory (derivative of ἐλέφας ‘elephant,’ not occurring in the NT) pertaining to consisting of the tusks of elephants Since ivory, whether of elephant or walrus tusks, is well known throughout the world, there are usually few problems involved in finding a satisfactory term. However, where a term for ‘ivory’ is not known, one can always speak of ‘beautiful bone.’

[REV 18:12](https://ref.ly/Jude18:12)

* **Associated Passages:** Revelation 18:12

## Ἕλλην (id: 001687000000000)

Ἕλλην
=====

Ἕλλην (G1672)

noun nom

11\.90 · Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes · Ethnic\-Cultural

civilized, Greek a person who participates in Greek culture and in so doing would speak the Greek language, but not necessarily a person of Greek ethnic background In [ROM 1:14](https://ref.ly/Acts1:14) it is possible that the contrast Ἕλλησίν τε καὶ βαρβάροις may be interpreted as ‘to the Greek\-speaking persons and to those who do not speak Greek,’ but the entire context of [ROM 1:14](https://ref.ly/Acts1:14) points to a somewhat broader designation.

[ROM 1:14](https://ref.ly/Acts1:14) · [1CO 1:22](https://ref.ly/Rom1:22) · [1CO 1:24](https://ref.ly/Rom1:24)

11\.40 · Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes · Socio\-Religious

Gentile, non\-Jew, Greek (an extended meaning of Ἕλλην\[a] ‘Greek,’ [11\.90](#11.90)) a person who is a Gentile in view of being a Greek Though the occurrence of Ἕλλην in [JHN 7:35](https://ref.ly/Luke7:35) could be interpreted as merely a reference to Greeks, it is probably a reference to Greeks as being Gentiles, and therefore Ἕλλην\[b] is classified here in the subdomain of socio\-religious groups. The occurrence of Ἕλλην in [ACT 14:1](https://ref.ly/John14:1) (ὥστε πιστεῦσαι Ἰουδαίων τε καὶ Ἑλλήνων πολὺ πλῆθος ‘so that a large number of Jews and Gentiles believed’) is another instance of Ἕλλην referring primarily to a socio\-religious group rather than merely to Greeks as a nation.

[JHN 7:35](https://ref.ly/Luke7:35) · [JHN 7:35](https://ref.ly/Luke7:35) · [JHN 12:20](https://ref.ly/Luke12:20) · [ACT 14:1](https://ref.ly/John14:1) · [ACT 16:1](https://ref.ly/John16:1) · [ACT 16:3](https://ref.ly/John16:3) · [ACT 17:4](https://ref.ly/John17:4) · [ACT 18:4](https://ref.ly/John18:4) · [ACT 19:10](https://ref.ly/John19:10) · [ACT 19:17](https://ref.ly/John19:17) · [ACT 20:21](https://ref.ly/John20:21) · [ACT 21:28](https://ref.ly/John21:28) · [ROM 1:16](https://ref.ly/Acts1:16) · [ROM 2:9](https://ref.ly/Acts2:9) · [ROM 2:10](https://ref.ly/Acts2:10) · [ROM 3:9](https://ref.ly/Acts3:9) · [ROM 10:12](https://ref.ly/Acts10:12) · [1CO 1:22](https://ref.ly/Rom1:22) · [1CO 1:24](https://ref.ly/Rom1:24) · [1CO 10:32](https://ref.ly/Rom10:32) · [1CO 12:13](https://ref.ly/Rom12:13) · [GAL 2:3](https://ref.ly/2Cor2:3) · [GAL 3:28](https://ref.ly/2Cor3:28) · [COL 3:11](https://ref.ly/Phil3:11)

* **Associated Passages:** Romans 1:14; 1 Corinthians 1:22; 1 Corinthians 1:24; John 7:35; John 12:20; Acts 14:1; Acts 16:1; Acts 16:3; Acts 17:4; Acts 18:4; Acts 19:10; Acts 19:17; Acts 20:21; Acts 21:28; Romans 1:16; Romans 2:9; Romans 2:10; Romans 3:9; Romans 10:12; 1 Corinthians 10:32; 1 Corinthians 12:13; Galatians 2:3; Galatians 3:28; Colossians 3:11


## Ἑλληνικός (id: 001688000000000)

Ἑλληνικός
=========

Ἑλληνικός (G1673)

adj

11\.92 · Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes · Ethnic\-Cultural

Greek pertaining to what is Greek In rendering this expression in [REV 9:11](https://ref.ly/Jude9:11) it is often necessary to say ‘in the language spoken by the Greeks’ or ‘in the Greek language.’ Ελληνικός as an adjective does not denote the Greek language as such, but in the feminine form the reference would be to γλῶσσα ‘language.’

[REV 9:11](https://ref.ly/Jude9:11)

* **Associated Passages:** Revelation 9:11


## Ἑλληνίς (id: 001689000000000)

Ἑλληνίς
=======

Ἑλληνίς (G1674)

noun nom

11\.91 · Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes · Ethnic\-Cultural

Greek woman a woman of Greek culture and language It is possible that in [MRK 7:26](https://ref.ly/Matt7:26) Ἑλληνίς is to be understood in the sense of Gentile (see [11\.41](#11.41)), for the account seems to focus upon the fact that this woman who was not a Jewess nevertheless displayed remarkable faith and confidence in Jesus’ ability to help.

[MRK 7:26](https://ref.ly/Matt7:26)

11\.41 · Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes · Socio\-Religious

Gentile woman, non\-Jew, Greek (an extended meaning of Ἑλληνίς\[a] ‘Greek woman,’ [11\.91](#11.91)) a female who is a Gentile in view of being a Greek Though one may translate Ἑλληνίς in [ACT 17:12](https://ref.ly/John17:12) as ‘Greek,’ the focus is probably upon these women as being Gentiles rather than merely Greeks, for it is the Gentile status of these persons which seems to have been particularly aggravating to some of the Jews in that region.

[ACT 17:12](https://ref.ly/John17:12)

* **Associated Passages:** Mark 7:26; Acts 17:12


## Ἑλληνιστής (id: 001690000000000)

Ἑλληνιστής
==========

Ἑλληνιστής (G1675)

noun nom

11\.93 · Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes · Ethnic\-Cultural

Greek\-speaking Jew a Greek\-speaking Jew in contrast to one speaking a Semitic language The Greek\-speaking Jews were basically Jewish in culture and religion, but they had adopted certain customs typical of the larger Greco\-Roman world in which many of them lived. This inevitably resulted in certain suspicions and misunderstandings.

[ACT 6:1](https://ref.ly/John6:1) · [ACT 9:29](https://ref.ly/John9:29) · [ACT 11:20](https://ref.ly/John11:20)

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 6:1; Acts 9:29; Acts 11:20

## ἐλλογέω (id: 001692000000000)

ἐλλογέω
=======

ἐλλογέω (G1677) — ἐλλογάω

33\.45 · Communication · Written Language

to record, to list to keep a record of something There may be difficulties involved in changing the passive expression ‘no account is kept of sins’ in [ROM 5:13](https://ref.ly/Acts5:13) to ‘God doesn’t keep an account of sins,’ for this might imply that God was unconcerned about sin. It is sometimes possible to avoid this difficulty by rendering ‘no account is kept of sins’ as ‘a sin is not listed as a sin.’

[ROM 5:13](https://ref.ly/Acts5:13)

57\.226 · Possess, Transfer, Exchange · Keep Records

to charge to charge to an account

92\.3 · Discourse Referentials · Speaker

myself, me a reflexive reference to the first person singular within the immediate context For the plural, see [92\.25](#92.25).

[MAT 8:9](https://ref.ly/Matt8:9) · [LUK 7:7](https://ref.ly/Mark7:7) · [LUK 7:8](https://ref.ly/Mark7:8) · [JHN 5:30](https://ref.ly/Luke5:30) · [JHN 5:31](https://ref.ly/Luke5:31) · [JHN 7:17](https://ref.ly/Luke7:17) · [JHN 7:28](https://ref.ly/Luke7:28) · [JHN 8:14](https://ref.ly/Luke8:14) · [JHN 8:18](https://ref.ly/Luke8:18) · [JHN 8:28](https://ref.ly/Luke8:28) · [JHN 8:42](https://ref.ly/Luke8:42) · [JHN 8:54](https://ref.ly/Luke8:54) · [JHN 10:18](https://ref.ly/Luke10:18) · [JHN 12:32](https://ref.ly/Luke12:32) · [JHN 12:49](https://ref.ly/Luke12:49) · [JHN 14:3](https://ref.ly/Luke14:3) · [JHN 14:10](https://ref.ly/Luke14:10) · [JHN 14:21](https://ref.ly/Luke14:21) · [JHN 17:19](https://ref.ly/Luke17:19) · [ACT 20:24](https://ref.ly/John20:24) · [ACT 24:10](https://ref.ly/John24:10) · [ACT 26:2](https://ref.ly/John26:2) · [ACT 26:9](https://ref.ly/John26:9) · [ROM 11:4](https://ref.ly/Acts11:4) · [1CO 4:3](https://ref.ly/Rom4:3) · [1CO 4:4](https://ref.ly/Rom4:4) · [1CO 4:6](https://ref.ly/Rom4:6) · [1CO 7:7](https://ref.ly/Rom7:7) · [1CO 9:19](https://ref.ly/Rom9:19) · [1CO 10:33](https://ref.ly/Rom10:33) · [2CO 2:1](https://ref.ly/1Cor2:1) · [2CO 11:7](https://ref.ly/1Cor11:7) · [2CO 11:9](https://ref.ly/1Cor11:9) · [2CO 12:5](https://ref.ly/1Cor12:5) · [GAL 2:18](https://ref.ly/2Cor2:18) · [PHP 3:13](https://ref.ly/Eph3:13) · [PHM 1:13](https://ref.ly/Titus1:13)

## ἐμβαίνω (id: 001699000000000)

ἐμβαίνω
=======

ἐμβαίνω (G1684)

βαίνω

15\.95 · Linear Movement · Come/Go Into

to embark, to get into a boat to go into or onto, as in the case of a boat In certain contexts ἐμβαίνω\[a] occurs without a specific indication of a ship or boat in the immediate context, for example, πάλιν ἐμβὰς ἀπῆλθεν εἰς τὸ πέραν ‘again embarking, he left for the other side’ [MRK 8:13](https://ref.ly/Matt8:13).\|In a number of languages it is necessary to introduce into the context some specific reference to a boat or ship.

[MAT 8:23](https://ref.ly/Matt8:23) · [MAT 9:1](https://ref.ly/Matt9:1) · [MAT 13:2](https://ref.ly/Matt13:2) · [MAT 14:22](https://ref.ly/Matt14:22) · [MAT 15:39](https://ref.ly/Matt15:39) · [MRK 4:1](https://ref.ly/Matt4:1) · [MRK 5:18](https://ref.ly/Matt5:18) · [MRK 6:45](https://ref.ly/Matt6:45) · [MRK 8:10](https://ref.ly/Matt8:10) · [MRK 8:13](https://ref.ly/Matt8:13) · [LUK 5:3](https://ref.ly/Mark5:3) · [LUK 8:22](https://ref.ly/Mark8:22) · [LUK 8:37](https://ref.ly/Mark8:37) · [JHN 6:17](https://ref.ly/Luke6:17) · [JHN 6:24](https://ref.ly/Luke6:24) · [JHN 21:3](https://ref.ly/Luke21:3)

15\.225 · Linear Movement · Walk, Step

to go into, to step into to step into some area This meaning is only found as a variant reading: ὁ οὖν πρῶτος ἐμβὰς μετὰ τὴν ταραχὴν τοῦ ὕδατος ὑγιὴς ἐγίνετο ‘the first one to go into (the pool) after the water was stirred up was healed’ [JHN 5:3](https://ref.ly/Luke5:3) (apparatus).

[JHN 5:3](https://ref.ly/Luke5:3)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 8:23; Matthew 9:1; Matthew 13:2; Matthew 14:22; Matthew 15:39; Mark 4:1; Mark 5:18; Mark 6:45; Mark 8:10; Mark 8:13; Luke 5:3; Luke 8:22; Luke 8:37; John 6:17; John 6:24; John 21:3; John 5:3

ἐμβάλλω
=======

ἐμβάλλω (G1685)

βάλλω

85\.35 · Existence in Space · Put, Place

to put into, to throw into to put or throw into a place Despite the use of the verb ἐμβάλλω in [LUK 12:5](https://ref.ly/Mark12:5), the focus of meaning is not so much upon the act of throwing or hurling but rather upon the act of causing someone to be in a particular place.

[LUK 12:5](https://ref.ly/Mark12:5)

## ἐμβάπτω (id: 001701000000000)

ἐμβάπτω
=======

βάπτω

47\.11 · Activities Involving Liquids or Masses · Use of Liquids

to dip in to dip an object in a liquid1

[MAT 26:23](https://ref.ly/Matt26:23) · [MRK 14:20](https://ref.ly/Matt14:20)

1 It is possible that ἐμβάπτω differs in meaning from βάπτω in specifying somewhat more clearly the factor of something being in something else.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 26:23; Mark 14:20


## ἐμβατεύω (id: 001702000000000)

ἐμβατεύω
========

ἐμβατεύω (G1687)

βαίνω

53\.15 · Religious Activities · Religious Practice

\[Glosses \- en] The meaning of ἐμβατεύω, which occurs only once in the NT, namely, in [COL 2:18](https://ref.ly/Phil2:18), is obscure. It may mean more or less literally ‘to set foot upon’ or ‘to enter’ or possibly ‘to come into possession of.’ It may also mean ‘to enter into’ in the sense of to go into detail in treating a subject, but it seems more likely that ἐμβατεύω in [COL 2:18](https://ref.ly/Phil2:18) is a technical term derived from the mystery religions, and it could be interpreted in the phrase ἅ ἑόρακεν ἐμβατεύων as meaning ‘who enters the sanctuary which he saw in ecstasy’ or ‘taking his stand on what he has seen in the mysteries.’ In view of the context, which speaks of someone being puffed up without cause, one might also render this Greek phrase as ‘by what he saw when he was initiated.’ For further data on this semantically difficult term, see A Greek\-English Lexicon of the New Testament, University of Chicago Press, third edition edited by Danker (BDAG).

[COL 2:18](https://ref.ly/Phil2:18)

* **Associated Passages:** Colossians 2:18


## ἐμβιβάζω (id: 001703000000000)

ἐμβιβάζω
========

ἐμβιβάζω (G1688)

βιβάζω

15\.96 · Linear Movement · Come/Go Into

to cause to go aboard, to cause to embark to cause someone to go into, as in the case of a boat The causative in this type of context may be expressed simply as ‘he commanded us,’ or the causative may be somewhat weakened to a form such as in English ‘he had us go aboard.’

[ACT 27:6](https://ref.ly/John27:6)

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 27:6


## ἐμβλέπω (id: 001704000000000)

ἐμβλέπω
=======

ἐμβλέπω (G1689)

βλέπω

24\.9 · Sensory Events and States · See

to look straight at, to look directly at to direct one’s vision and attention to a particular object In [JHN 1:36](https://ref.ly/Luke1:36) ἐμβλέπω may not differ significantly in meaning from βλέπω in [JHN 1:29](https://ref.ly/Luke1:29) (see [24\.7](#24.7)).

[MAT 6:26](https://ref.ly/Matt6:26)2 · [MAT 19:26](https://ref.ly/Matt19:26) · [MRK 8:25](https://ref.ly/Matt8:25) · [MRK 10:21](https://ref.ly/Matt10:21) · [MRK 10:27](https://ref.ly/Matt10:27) · [MRK 14:67](https://ref.ly/Matt14:67) · [LUK 20:17](https://ref.ly/Mark20:17) · [LUK 22:61](https://ref.ly/Mark22:61) · [JHN 1:36](https://ref.ly/Luke1:36) · [JHN 1:42](https://ref.ly/Luke1:42) · [ACT 22:11](https://ref.ly/John22:11)

30\.1 · Think · To Think, Thought

to think about, to consider to process information by giving consideration to various aspects1 In a number of languages the concept of thinking is closely related to terms referring to perception. For example, in some languages one may speak of thinking as ‘to see with the heart’ or ‘to look at with the liver.’

[MAT 6:26](https://ref.ly/Matt6:26)2

1 It is possible that ἐμβλέπω\[b] differs somewhat in meaning from βλέπω\[d] in focusing the thought processes on some particular object or matter, but it is difficult to determine this on the basis of existing contexts. 2 MAT 6:26 It is possible to regard ἐμβλέπω in [MAT 6:26](https://ref.ly/Matt6:26) as involving a combination of seeing (see [24\.9](#24.9)) plus intellectual activity.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 6:26; Matthew 19:26; Mark 8:25; Mark 10:21; Mark 10:27; Mark 14:67; Luke 20:17; Luke 22:61; John 1:36; John 1:42; Acts 22:11

## ἐμέω (id: 001706000000000)

ἐμέω
====

ἐμέω (G1692)

23\.44 · Physiological Processes and States · Processes Involving the Mouth, Other Than Eating and Drinking

to vomit Since a term meaning ‘to vomit’ often carries somewhat vulgar connotations, ἐμέω in [REV 3:16](https://ref.ly/Jude3:16) has frequently been translated as ‘to spit out of my mouth.’\|It is also possible to interpret ‘to vomit out of the mouth’ as an idiom meaning ‘to reject.’1

[REV 3:16](https://ref.ly/Jude3:16)

1 Though it is possible to understand the expression vomit out of the mouth as being an idiom meaning to reject, it is probably better to assume that this expression (ἐμέω ἐκ τοῦ στόματος) is simply used figuratively rather than being a conventionalized idiom.

* **Associated Passages:** Revelation 3:16

μαίνομαι

88\.182 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Anger, Be Indignant With

to be enraged, to be infuriated, to be insanely angry to be so furiously angry with someone as to be almost out of one’s mind In some instances it may be possible to render ἐμμαίνομαι in [ACT 26:11](https://ref.ly/John26:11) as ‘to be so angry as to be like an insane person.’

[ACT 26:11](https://ref.ly/John26:11)

## ἐμμένω (id: 001710000000000)

ἐμμένω
======

ἐμμένω (G1696)

μένω

85\.55 · Existence in Space · Remain, Stay

to remain, to stay to remain in the same place over a period of time1

[ACT 28:30](https://ref.ly/John28:30)

68\.11 · Aspect · Continue

to continue, to remain in, to keep on to continue in an activity or state In [HEB 8:9](https://ref.ly/Phlm8:9) the implication of ‘to continue in a covenant’ is to adhere to the articles or stipulations of a covenant. Accordingly, one may render [HEB 8:9](https://ref.ly/Phlm8:9) as ‘because they did not remain faithful to my covenant.’ In [ACT 14:22](https://ref.ly/John14:22) (παρακαλοῦντες ἐμμένειν τῇ πίστει ‘encourage them to remain true to the faith’), it is also possible to translate the expression ‘to remain true to the faith’ as ‘to continue to believe.’

[ACT 14:22](https://ref.ly/John14:22) · [GAL 3:10](https://ref.ly/2Cor3:10) · [HEB 8:9](https://ref.ly/Phlm8:9)

1 There may be slight nuances of meaning in the various terms in this series, but such distinctions cannot be clearly determined from existing contexts, especially since the prefixes are redundant with other expressions of space.

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 28:30; Acts 14:22; Galatians 3:10; Hebrews 8:9

=======

33\.406 · Communication · Mock, Ridicule

to mock, to ridicule to make fun of someone by pretending that he is not what he is or by imitating him in a distorted manner

[MAT 20:19](https://ref.ly/Matt20:19) · [MAT 27:29](https://ref.ly/Matt27:29) · [MAT 27:31](https://ref.ly/Matt27:31) · [MAT 27:41](https://ref.ly/Matt27:41) · [MRK 10:34](https://ref.ly/Matt10:34) · [MRK 15:20](https://ref.ly/Matt15:20) · [MRK 15:31](https://ref.ly/Matt15:31) · [LUK 14:29](https://ref.ly/Mark14:29) · [LUK 18:32](https://ref.ly/Mark18:32) · [LUK 22:63](https://ref.ly/Mark22:63) · [LUK 23:11](https://ref.ly/Mark23:11) · [LUK 23:36](https://ref.ly/Mark23:36)

88\.156 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Mislead, Lead Astray, Deceive

to trick, to make a fool of to trick someone into thinking or doing something and thus to make a fool of such a person In [MAT 2:16](https://ref.ly/Matt2:16) ἐμπαίζω would seem to carry two closely related meanings, namely ‘to deceive’ and also ‘to make a fool of,’ and therefore it may be quite appropriate to translate this portion of [MAT 2:16](https://ref.ly/Matt2:16) as ‘when Herod realized that the wise men had deceived him and made a fool of him.’

[MAT 2:16](https://ref.ly/Matt2:16)

## ἐμπίπλημι (id: 001718000000000)

ἐμπίπλημι
=========

ἐμπίπλημι (G1705) — ἐμπίμπλαμαι, ἐμπίμπλαμαι, ἐμπιμπλάομαι, ἐμπίμπλημι, ἐμπίπλαμαι, ἐμπίπλαμαι, ἐμπιπλάω

πίπλημι

23\.18 · Physiological Processes and States · Eat, Drink

**Collocation:** or ἐμπιπλάομαι · ἐμπί(μ)πλαμαι

to be satisfied, to have one’s fill (two different forms of the same base, compare [23\.17](#23.17)) to be satisfied with food on the basis of having eaten enough See also comments at [23\.17](#23.17).

25\.114 · Attitudes and Emotions · Enjoy, Take Pleasure In, Be Fond of Doing

[ROM 15:24](https://ref.ly/Acts15:24)

23\.17 · Physiological Processes and States · Eat, Drink

**Collocation:** ἐμπί(μ)πλημι · ἐμπιπλάω

to satisfy with food, to fill with food (two different forms of the same base, compare [23\.18](#23.18)) to cause someone to be satisfied as the result of food which has been provided in sufficient quantity It is possible that in [LUK 1:53](https://ref.ly/Mark1:53) the reference is to God’s providence in general rather than merely to feeding the hungry.\|A strictly literal rendering of ἐμπίμπλημι or ἐμπιπλάω in the sense of ‘to fill with food’ might carry the wrong implication, namely, ‘to stuff with food’ to the point of making someone extremely uncomfortable. It may therefore be important to divide somewhat the complex semantic components of meaning and to translate ‘to cause to eat much and to become happy’ or ‘to cause to eat enough and as a result to be content.’

[LUK 1:53](https://ref.ly/Mark1:53) · [ACT 14:17](https://ref.ly/John14:17)

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 6:25; John 6:12; Romans 15:24; Luke 1:53; Acts 14:17


## ἐμπίπρημι (id: 001719000000000)

ἐμπίπρημι
=========

ἐμπίπρημι (G1714) — ἐμπίμπρημι

πίπρημι

14\.65 · Physical Events and States · Burning

to ignite, to kindle, to set ablaze, to start a fire, to light a lamp to cause the process of burning to begin As already noted in the series of terms listed in [14\.63](#14.63), languages differ appreciably in terms employed for different phases and results of burning. For example, to set something on fire for the sake of providing warmth may be expressed by quite a different term from that which is used in speaking of the process of setting something on fire in order to destroy it.\|ἐμπίπρημι refers specifically to the process of setting something on fire, but obviously the implication in [MAT 22:7](https://ref.ly/Matt22:7) is that the city was burned down. A number of translations, therefore, follow the implied component of ἐμπίπρημι and translate ‘he burned down their city.’ In some languages it may seem strange to speak of ‘burning down a city.’ A more natural expression would be ‘to burn down all the buildings of the city.’

[MAT 22:7](https://ref.ly/Matt22:7)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 22:7

## ἐμπλέκω (id: 001721000000000)

ἐμπλέκω
=======

ἐμπλέκω (G1707) — ἐμπλέκομαι

πλέκω

90\.81 · Case · Experiencer

to become involved (a figurative extension of meaning of ἐμπλέκομαι ‘to become entangled,’ not occurring in the NT) to become so involved in some activity as to experience severe restrictions as to what one can do

[2TI 2:4](https://ref.ly/1Tim2:4)1 · [2PE 2:20](https://ref.ly/1Pet2:20)

1 2TI 2:4 As in the case of πράσσω\[d] ([90\.76](#90.76) and footnote), ἐμπλέκομαι in [2TI 2:4](https://ref.ly/1Tim2:4) involves both action and experiencing. One may say, therefore, that ἐμπλέκομαι in such a context is semantically complex in that it really belongs to two different semantic domains. Here, however, the condition of the soldier seems to be more in focus than his activity, and for that reason ἐμπλέκομαι is classified in this subdomain.

* **Associated Passages:** 2 Timothy 2:4; 2 Peter 2:20

## ἐμπτύω (id: 001729000000000)

ἐμπτύω
======

ἐμπτύω (G1716)

πτύω

23\.43 · Physiological Processes and States · Processes Involving the Mouth, Other Than Eating and Drinking

to spit (on) to spit on or at something or someone1 In some societies spitting has quite a different symbolic meaning than it has in the Western world. In fact, in some societies in Africa, to spit upon a person’s head is to confer a blessing. It may therefore be necessary in the case of [MAT 26:67](https://ref.ly/Matt26:67) to translate ‘they spat in his face’ as ‘they spat in his face to dishonor him’ or ‘... to show their anger against him.’

[MAT 26:67](https://ref.ly/Matt26:67) · [MAT 27:30](https://ref.ly/Matt27:30) · [MRK 10:34](https://ref.ly/Matt10:34) · [MRK 14:65](https://ref.ly/Matt14:65) · [MRK 15:19](https://ref.ly/Matt15:19) · [LUK 18:32](https://ref.ly/Mark18:32)

1 It is possible that a distinction in meaning should be made between πτύω and ἐμπτύω, in which case the latter term would emphasize the spitting in or upon an object. However, there are insufficient contrasts in contexts to warrant making such a distinction.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 26:67; Matthew 27:30; Mark 10:34; Mark 14:65; Mark 15:19; Luke 18:32

28\.36 · Know · Well Known, Clearly Shown, Revealed

1 All of these meanings involve a shift from the sensory domain of seeing, causing to see, or giving light to, to the cognitive domain of making something fully known, evident, and clear. 2 JHN 14:21 In [JHN 14:21](https://ref.ly/Luke14:21) it is difficult to know precisely the manner or extent of the revealing represented by the verb ἐμφανίζω. It would seem that cognitive awareness or knowledge about is far more likely than any physical or visual appearance. 3 ἀγγέλλω appears to be more generic in meaning than a term such as ἐμφανίζω\[c] ([33\.208](#33.208)), but it is difficult to indicate in all instances the various aspects of denotation and connotation associated with the meanings in this subdomain.

## ἐμφυσάω (id: 001733000000000)

ἐμφυσάω
=======

ἐμφυσάω (G1720)

φυσάω

23\.185 · Physiological Processes and States · Breathe, Breath

to breathe on to breathe upon something The process of breathing on someone may have very important symbolic implications. In some instances this can be related to a blessing, as in [JHN 20:22](https://ref.ly/Luke20:22), but in some languages the act of breathing on a person almost inevitably suggests some harmful influence, often connected with the use of black magic. It may therefore be important to select some less specific term so as to avoid a symbolic meaning which would considerably distort the significance of this one passage in which ἐμφυσάω occurs in the NT.

[JHN 20:22](https://ref.ly/Luke20:22)

* **Associated Passages:** John 20:22

83\.47 · Spacial Positions · On, Upon, On the Surface Of

on, at a position on the surface of an area In a number of languages it may be quite impossible to speak of ‘tablets of the human heart’ ([2CO 3:3](https://ref.ly/1Cor3:3)). An equivalent may be ‘in the human heart’ or ‘in people’s hearts.’ In some languages, however, it may be necessary to substitute ‘mind,’ for example, ‘in people’s minds.’

83\.23 · Spacial Positions · At, Beside, Near, Far

84\.22 · Spacial Extensions · Extension To a Goal

into extension toward a goal which is inside an area This meaning is also found as a variant reading: ἄγγελος γὰρ κυρίου κατὰ καιρὸν κατέβαινεν ἐν τῇ κολυμβήθρᾳ ‘an angel of the Lord from time to time went down into the pool’ [JHN 5:3](https://ref.ly/Luke5:3) (apparatus).

89\.119 · Relations · Association

89\.141 · Relations · Substance

[ACT 7:14](https://ref.ly/John7:14) · [ROM 5:15](https://ref.ly/Acts5:15) · [1CO 4:20](https://ref.ly/Rom4:20) · [1CO 4:20](https://ref.ly/Rom4:20) · [EPH 2:15](https://ref.ly/Gal2:15) · [EPH 5:9](https://ref.ly/Gal5:9) · [EPH 6:15](https://ref.ly/Gal6:15) · [COL 1:10](https://ref.ly/Phil1:10) · [COL 2:11](https://ref.ly/Phil2:11) · [TIT 1:9](https://ref.ly/2Tim1:9) · [1JN 4:10](https://ref.ly/2Pet4:10)

90\.56 · Case · Experiencer

in relation to, with respect to, to a marker of an experiencer of an event5 It is also possible to interpret the relation expressed by ἐν in [GAL 1:16](https://ref.ly/2Cor1:16) as indicating agent or instrument, for example, ‘to reveal his Son by me’ or ‘... through my life’ (compare ἐν as an expression of agent in [90\.6](#90.6) and instrument in [90\.10](#90.10)), or even means (ἐν ἐμοί referring to his behavior) as in [89\.76](#89.76).

90\.6 · Case · Agent, Personal or Nonpersonal, Causative or Immediate, Direct or Indirect

by, from (with the dative) a marker of agent, often with the implication of an agent being used as an instrument, and in some instances relating to general behavior rather than to some specific event

90\.30 · Case · Guarantor Participant with Oaths

89\.26 · Relations · Cause and/or Reason

89\.48 · Relations · Result

with the result that, so that as a result, to cause a marker of result, with the probable implication of a preceding process In some languages this expression in [LUK 1:17](https://ref.ly/Mark1:17) may be rendered as ‘to cause the disobedient people to think like the righteous.’

90\.23 · Case · Content

concerning, with respect to, with reference to, about, in (with the dative) a marker of content as a means of specifying a particular referent

70\.4 · Real, Unreal

**Collocation:** ἐν ἀληθείᾳ

actually, really (an idiom: in truth) pertaining to being a real or actual event or state8

[MAT 22:16](https://ref.ly/Matt22:16) · [JHN 17:19](https://ref.ly/Luke17:19) · [2CO 7:14](https://ref.ly/1Cor7:14) · [COL 1:6](https://ref.ly/Phil1:6) · [2JN 1:1](https://ref.ly/1John1:1) · [2JN 1:4](https://ref.ly/1John1:4) · [3JN 1:1](https://ref.ly/2John1:1)

65\.56 · Value · Important, Unimportant

**Collocation:** ἐν βάρει εἰμί

to insist on one’s worth, to claim one’s importance (an idiom, literally: to be in weight, see [86\.1](#86.1)) to insist on one’s importance or worth It is possible to interpret ἐν βάρει εἰμί as denoting a person’s status, and accordingly, one could classify this idiom in Domain 87 Status, but it is also possible to interpret this expression in [1TH 2:7](https://ref.ly/Col2:7) as the demands which could be placed upon persons, and therefore one could translate as ‘we could have made demands on you.’ Such a translation would imply the relationship of authority, and the meaning could be classified in Domain 37 Control, Rule.

[1TH 2:7](https://ref.ly/Col2:7)

23\.50 · Physiological Processes and States · Birth, Procreation

**Collocation:** ἐν γαστρὶ ἔχω

pregnant, to be pregnant (an idiom, literally: to have in the womb) to be in a state of pregnancy This expression contrasts with the meaning of συλλαμβάνω\[b], [23\.49](#23.49), in that it seems to focus more upon the state of pregnancy rather than the event of conception.\|It is impossible to determine whether in [MAT 1:23](https://ref.ly/Matt1:23) (ἐν γαστρὶ ἕξει καὶ τέξεται υἱόν ‘she will be pregnant and bear a son’) the focus is upon the state of pregnancy or the fact of conception (see [23\.49](#23.49)).

[MAT 1:18](https://ref.ly/Matt1:18) · [MAT 1:23](https://ref.ly/Matt1:23) · [MAT 24:19](https://ref.ly/Matt24:19) · [MRK 13:17](https://ref.ly/Matt13:17) · [LUK 21:23](https://ref.ly/Mark21:23) · [1TH 5:3](https://ref.ly/Col5:3) · [REV 12:2](https://ref.ly/Jude12:2)

67\.89 · Time · Duration of Time without Reference to Points or Units of Time: Time, Spend Time, Always, Eternal, Old, Immediately, Young

**Collocation:** ἐν μεγάλῳ

long, long time, for some time (an idiom, literally: in great) pertaining to a relatively long duration of time

[ACT 26:29](https://ref.ly/John26:29)

67\.106 · Time · Duration of Time without Reference to Points or Units of Time: Time, Spend Time, Always, Eternal, Old, Immediately, Young

**Collocation:** ἐν ὀλίγῳ

a little while, for a little while, a short time, brief, briefly ἐν ὀλίγῳ \- pertaining to a relatively brief extent of time9 It is also possible to interpret ἐν ὀλίγῳ in [ACT 26:28](https://ref.ly/John26:28) as meaning ‘easily’ or ‘without difficulty’ (see [22\.41](#22.41)).

[ACT 26:28](https://ref.ly/John26:28) · [ACT 26:29](https://ref.ly/John26:29) · [EPH 3:3](https://ref.ly/Gal3:3)

22\.41 · Trouble, Hardship, Relief, Favorable Circumstances · Easy, Light

**Collocation:** ἐν ὀλίγῳ

easily, without much trouble, without difficulty pertaining to that which is accomplished easily or without difficulty For another, more probable, interpretation of ἐν ὀλίγῳ in [ACT 26:28](https://ref.ly/John26:28), see [67\.106](#67.106).

[ACT 26:28](https://ref.ly/John26:28) · [ACT 26:29](https://ref.ly/John26:29)

33\.17 · Communication · Discourse Types

**Collocation:** ἐν παραβολῇ

symbolically, allegorically, figuratively, so to speak pertaining to expressions which are not to be understood literally, but symbolically or figuratively In a number of languages it may be more satisfactory to translate ἐν παραβολῇ and πνευματικῶς\[c] by a negative expression, for example, ‘not literally’ or ‘not really.’ The expression in [REV 11:8](https://ref.ly/Jude11:8) might be translated as ‘that city might be called Sodom, but it isn’t really Sodom.’

[HEB 11:19](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:19)

28\.29 · Know · Well Known, Clearly Shown, Revealed

**Collocation:** ἐν παρρησίᾳ

publicly, in an evident manner, well known (an idiom, literally: in boldness) in an evident or publicly known manner

[JHN 7:4](https://ref.ly/Luke7:4) · [COL 2:15](https://ref.ly/Phil2:15)

67\.114 · Time · Duration of Time without Reference to Points or Units of Time: Time, Spend Time, Always, Eternal, Old, Immediately, Young

**Collocation:** ἐν ῥιπῇ ὀφθαλμοῦ

quickly, suddenly (an idiom, literally: in the blinking of an eye) an extremely short duration of time For another interpretation of the phrase ἐν ῥιπῇ ὀφθαλμοῦ, see [16\.5](#16.5).

[1CO 15:52](https://ref.ly/Rom15:52)

67\.111 · Time · Duration of Time without Reference to Points or Units of Time: Time, Spend Time, Always, Eternal, Old, Immediately, Young

**Collocation:** ἐν τάχει

a very short while, quickly (in combination with the preposition ἐν) a very brief period of time In [ACT 22:18](https://ref.ly/John22:18) τάχος evidently refers to a brief period of time before fulfilling the admonition to leave Jerusalem.

[LUK 18:8](https://ref.ly/Mark18:8) · [ACT 12:7](https://ref.ly/John12:7) · [ACT 22:18](https://ref.ly/John22:18) · [ACT 25:4](https://ref.ly/John25:4) · [ROM 16:20](https://ref.ly/Acts16:20) · [1TI 3:14](https://ref.ly/2Thess3:14) · [REV 1:1](https://ref.ly/Jude1:1) · [REV 22:6](https://ref.ly/Jude22:6)

28\.71 · Know · Not Able To Be Known, Secret

**Collocation:** ἐν τῇ σκοτίᾳ

in secret, in private, secretly, privately (an idiom, literally: in the darkness) pertaining to not being able to be known by the public but known by some in\-group or by those immediately involved

[MAT 10:27](https://ref.ly/Matt10:27) · [LUK 12:3](https://ref.ly/Mark12:3)

67\.52 · Time · A Point of Time with Reference to Other Points of Time: Before, Long Ago, Now, At the Same Time, When, About, After

**Collocation:** ἐν τῷ (καθ)εξῆς

later, next (an idiom, literally: in the next) a point of time subsequent to another point of time, but with the implication of an ordered sequence

[LUK 7:11](https://ref.ly/Mark7:11) · [LUK 8:1](https://ref.ly/Mark8:1)

28\.71 · Know · Not Able To Be Known, Secret

**Collocation:** ἐν τῷ κρυπτῷ

in secret, in private, secretly, privately (an idiom, literally: in the hidden) pertaining to not being able to be known by the public but known by some in\-group or by those immediately involved

[MAT 6:4](https://ref.ly/Matt6:4) · [MAT 6:4](https://ref.ly/Matt6:4) · [MAT 6:6](https://ref.ly/Matt6:6) · [MAT 6:6](https://ref.ly/Matt6:6) · [JHN 7:4](https://ref.ly/Luke7:4) · [JHN 7:10](https://ref.ly/Luke7:10) · [JHN 18:20](https://ref.ly/Luke18:20)

67\.138 · Time · Duration of Time with Reference to Some Unit of Time: During, In, While, Throughout

**Collocation:** ἐν τῷ μεταξύ

in the meantime, meanwhile (an idiom, literally: in the between) an extent of time within another unit or extension of time

[JHN 4:31](https://ref.ly/Luke4:31)

28\.64 · Know · Able To Be Known

**Collocation:** ἐν τῷ φωτί

in public, publicly (an idiom, literally: in the light) pertaining to being widely known in view of the events in question having taken place in public10

[MAT 10:27](https://ref.ly/Matt10:27) · [LUK 12:3](https://ref.ly/Mark12:3)

67\.139 · Time · Duration of Time with Reference to Some Unit of Time: During, In, While, Throughout

**Collocation:** ἐν ᾧ

as long as, while an extent of time of the same length as another extent or unit of time

[MRK 2:19](https://ref.ly/Matt2:19) · [LUK 5:34](https://ref.ly/Mark5:34) · [JHN 5:7](https://ref.ly/Luke5:7) · [1PE 2:12](https://ref.ly/Jas2:12) · [1PE 3:16](https://ref.ly/Jas3:16)

1 In contexts where an accompanying expression designates movement into an area, ἐν\[a] and εἰς\[d] may be rendered as ‘into.’ 2 There are no doubt certain subtle distinctions in the meanings of the terms in [83\.9](#83.9). For example, πρός\[d] may suggest some kind of interaction or response and παρά\[b] may focus upon association, but it is difficult, if not impossible, on the basis of existing contexts to define precisely the differences in meaning. 3 Attendant circumstances are often expressed in English by participial or gerund phrases. 4 Manner is expressed in a number of different ways in diverse languages. In English it is often expressed by means of a phrase introduced by with, but the appropriate equivalent in some contexts is simply an adverb. Often, however, an entire clause or additional complete sentence is required. 5 As will be noted in a number of instances of markers of experiencer relations, there may be no lexical item in a translation which specifically corresponds to such a marker. 6 There are probably certain subtle distinctions between the use of διά\[c] in contrast with εἰς\[h] or ἐν\[p] as markers of means, but this cannot be clearly determined from existing contexts. 7 The difference in meaning between the set κατά\[g], ἐπί\[s], ἐν\[s], ἐκ\[k], and καθώς\[d] and the set πότε, ποτέ\[b], ὁπότε, ὅτε\[a], and ὅταν\[b] ([67\.30](#67.30)) is largely a matter of class meanings, but there may be certain subtle rhetorical differences as well. 8 It would seem that the meanings of items [70\.1](#70.1)\-[70\.4](#70.4) do reflect certain subtle differences, but it is not at all easy to determine precisely how they differ. 9 The series of expressions indicating relative shortness of time may very well differ slightly, at least in connotative meaning, but on the basis of existing NT contexts it is not possible to determine significant denotative differences. The phrases in this series may be technically regarded as idioms, but functionally they are better classed as frozen phrases, which act essentially like morphological units. 10 It is very possible that the two idioms in [28\.64](#28.64) differ somewhat in meaning, or at least in connotation, but it is difficult, if not impossible, to show this from existing contexts. 11 NEW NOTE en
