94\.10

**Collocation:** ἔρημος τῆς Ἰουδαίας

wilderness of Judea

[MAT 3:1](https://ref.ly/Matt3:1)

35\.55 · Help, Care For · Desert, Forsake

deserted, forsaken the state of a person who has been deserted or forsaken In rendering ἔρημος in [GAL 4:27](https://ref.ly/2Cor4:27) it may be important to indicate clearly that ‘the woman who was deserted’ refers to one who has been deserted by her husband.

[GAL 4:27](https://ref.ly/2Cor4:27)

[MAT 12:25](https://ref.ly/Matt12:25) · [LUK 11:17](https://ref.ly/Mark11:17) · [REV 17:16](https://ref.ly/Jude17:16) · [REV 18:17](https://ref.ly/Jude18:17) · [REV 18:19](https://ref.ly/Jude18:19)

## ἐρήμωσις (id: 002066000000000)

ἐρήμωσις
========

ἐρήμωσις (G2050)

noun · ἐρημόομαι

20\.41 · Violence, Harm, Destroy, Kill · Destroy

destroyed, destruction, desolation suffering destruction, with the implication of being deserted and abandoned

[LUK 21:20](https://ref.ly/Mark21:20)

53\.38 · Religious Activities · Defile, Unclean, Common

**Collocation:** τὸ βδέλυγμα τῆς ἐρημώσεως

an abomination which desolates, a horrible thing which defiles (a fixed phrase derived from Hebrew, literally: the detestable thing of desolation) an abomination (either an object or an event) which defiles a holy place and thus causes it to be abandoned and left desolate In translating βδέλυγμα, it may be necessary in some languages to use a phrase such as ‘that which God detests’ or ‘that which God hates’ or even ‘that which causes God’s anger.’ The term ἐρημώσεως may then be translated as ‘that which causes people to abandon’ or ‘that which causes something to be deserted.’ The entire phrase may then be translated in some languages as ‘that which God detests and which causes something to be abandoned’ or ‘... left desolate.’

[MAT 24:15](https://ref.ly/Matt24:15) · [MRK 13:14](https://ref.ly/Matt13:14)

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 21:20; Matthew 24:15; Mark 13:14

noun · ἐρίζω

88\.167 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Resentful, Hold a Grudge Against

selfish ambition, rivalry, resentfulness a feeling of resentfulness based upon jealousy and implying rivalry For another interpretation of ἐριθεία in [PHP 1:17](https://ref.ly/Eph1:17), see [39\.7](#39.7).\|The meaning of ‘rivalry’ may be expressed as ‘wanting to be better than someone else’ or ‘wanting to make people think they are better.’ The meaning of ‘selfish ambition’ may be rendered as ‘what they do is just to make themselves look bigger’ or ‘what they do is just for themselves.’

[ROM 2:8](https://ref.ly/Acts2:8) · [2CO 12:20](https://ref.ly/1Cor12:20) · [GAL 5:20](https://ref.ly/2Cor5:20) · [PHP 1:17](https://ref.ly/Eph1:17) · [PHP 2:3](https://ref.ly/Eph2:3) · [JAS 3:14](https://ref.ly/Heb3:14) · [JAS 3:16](https://ref.ly/Heb3:16)

39\.7 · Hostility, Strife · Opposition, Hostility

hostility, being against a feeling of hostility or opposition It is also possible to understand ἐριθεία in [PHP 1:17](https://ref.ly/Eph1:17) as meaning ‘selfish ambition’ or ‘rivalry’ (see [88\.167](#88.167)).

[PHP 1:17](https://ref.ly/Eph1:17) · [JAS 3:14](https://ref.ly/Heb3:14) · [JAS 3:16](https://ref.ly/Heb3:16)

* **Associated Passages:** Romans 2:8; 2 Corinthians 12:20; Galatians 5:20; Philippians 1:17; Philippians 2:3; James 3:14; James 3:16


## ἔριον (id: 002069000000000)

ἔριον
=====

ἔριον (G2053)

noun

8\.15 · Body, Body Parts, and Body Products · Parts of the Body

wool the curly hair that forms the fleece of sheep ἔριον in [REV 1:14](https://ref.ly/Jude1:14) and [HEB 9:19](https://ref.ly/Phlm9:19) may also mean unspun wool; hence ἔριον as ‘processed wool’ has been classified as an artifact ([6\.171](#6.171)).

[HEB 9:19](https://ref.ly/Phlm9:19) · [REV 1:14](https://ref.ly/Jude1:14)

6\.171 · Artifacts · Cloth, Leather, and Objects Made of Such Materials

wool the processed hair of sheep See also ἔριον\[a] in [8\.15](#8.15).

[HEB 9:19](https://ref.ly/Phlm9:19)

* **Associated Passages:** Hebrews 9:19; Revelation 1:14


## ἔρις (id: 002070000000000)

ἔρις
====

ἔρις (G2054)

noun · ἐρίζω

39\.22 · Hostility, Strife · Strife, Struggle

strife, discord conflict resulting from rivalry and discord1 In a number of languages the type of strife referred to by ἔρις\[a] is frequently described as verbal, for example, ‘always saying bad things about one another’ or ‘never having a good word to say to one another.’ See also [33\.447](#33.447).

[ROM 1:29](https://ref.ly/Acts1:29) · [ROM 13:13](https://ref.ly/Acts13:13) · [1CO 3:3](https://ref.ly/Rom3:3) · [2CO 12:20](https://ref.ly/1Cor12:20) · [GAL 5:20](https://ref.ly/2Cor5:20) · [PHP 1:15](https://ref.ly/Eph1:15)

33\.447 · Communication · Argue, Quarrel

to argue, quarrel, dispute to express differences of opinion, with at least some measure of antagonism or hostility In a number of contexts in which ἔρις occurs, it is difficult to determine whether there is definite verbal involvement or whether the reference is essentially to a state of rivalry or strife (see [39\.22](#39.22)).

[ROM 1:29](https://ref.ly/Acts1:29) · [1CO 1:11](https://ref.ly/Rom1:11) · [1CO 3:3](https://ref.ly/Rom3:3) · [1TI 6:4](https://ref.ly/2Thess6:4) · [TIT 3:9](https://ref.ly/2Tim3:9)

1 It is possible that in all instances of ἔρις\[a] the nature of the conflict is specifically verbal, and therefore may be regarded as essentially equivalent to ἔρις\[b] quarreling ([33\.447](#33.447)).

* **Associated Passages:** Romans 1:29; Romans 13:13; 1 Corinthians 3:3; 2 Corinthians 12:20; Galatians 5:20; Philippians 1:15; 1 Corinthians 1:11; 1 Timothy 6:4; Titus 3:9


## ἐρίφιον (id: 002071000000000)

ἐρίφιον
=======

ἐρίφιον (G2055)

noun · ἔριφος

4\.19 · Animals · Animals

kid, he\-goat, goat (the diminutive of ἔριφος but without special diminutive meaning1) kid, he\-goat in the singular and goats in the plural (in the plural form both male and female goats are included in the reference) Since goats are so widely distributed throughout the world, there is usually little or no difficulty involved in finding a satisfactory term. The difficulty arises, however, in the connotations of the term for goat, especially in contrast with sheep. In contrast with usage in the Bible, in many parts of the tropical world goats are much more highly prized than sheep, because they can forage well for themselves and are appreciated for their meat. Sheep, on the other hand, are often regarded as scavengers and are much less valued. One should not reverse biblical statements about sheep and goats, but marginal notes and a fuller explanation of cultural differences in a glossary are important.

[MAT 25:33](https://ref.ly/Matt25:33)

1 With a number of diminutive forms for animals there may be connotative meanings of endearment or special appreciation, but it is difficult to establish such values in the case of most contexts.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 25:33


## ἔριφος (id: 002072000000000)

ἔριφος
======

ἔριφος (G2056)

noun · ἐρίφιον

4\.19 · Animals · Animals

kid, he\-goat, goat kid, he\-goat in the singular and goats in the plural (in the plural form both male and female goats are included in the reference) Since goats are so widely distributed throughout the world, there is usually little or no difficulty involved in finding a satisfactory term. The difficulty arises, however, in the connotations of the term for goat, especially in contrast with sheep. In contrast with usage in the Bible, in many parts of the tropical world goats are much more highly prized than sheep, because they can forage well for themselves and are appreciated for their meat. Sheep, on the other hand, are often regarded as scavengers and are much less valued. One should not reverse biblical statements about sheep and goats, but marginal notes and a fuller explanation of cultural differences in a glossary are important.

[MAT 25:32](https://ref.ly/Matt25:32) · [LUK 15:29](https://ref.ly/Mark15:29)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 25:32; Luke 15:29

========

33\.145 · Communication · Interpret, Mean, Explain

to translate, to interpret to translate from one language to another In a number of languages the equivalent of ‘to translate’ or ‘to interpret’ is an idiomatic expression, for example, ‘it comes out in our language as’ or ‘in our words it means’ or ‘in our mouths it says.’

## ἑρπετόν (id: 002078000000000)

ἑρπετόν
=======

ἑρπετόν (G2062)

noun

4\.51 · Animals · Reptiles and Other 'Creeping Things'

creeping animal, reptile, snake general word for reptile that includes snakes and lizards Though ἑρπετόν is often interpreted as referring only to snakes, it also includes in biblical contexts (as the result of the influence of classifications based on Hebrew terminology, as in [GEN 1:25](https://ref.ly/Gen1:25) [GEN 1:26](https://ref.ly/Gen1:26) [GEN 1:30](https://ref.ly/Gen1:30) a number of small four\-footed animals as well as snakes, for example, rats, mice, frogs, toads, salamanders, and lizards. However, in the various NT contexts (for example [ACT 10:12](https://ref.ly/John10:12) [ACT 11:6](https://ref.ly/John11:6) [ROM 1:23](https://ref.ly/Acts1:23) [JAS 3:7](https://ref.ly/Heb3:7) where ‘creeping things’ are contrasted with birds, animals, and fish) it is probably more satisfactory to use a term which designates primarily snakes. In a number of languages a very important distinction is made between four\-footed reptiles (such as lizards) and snakes. If such a distinction is made, it is then probably better to use a term which designates only snakes, since a term for ‘animals’ will probably also include lizards.

[ACT 10:12](https://ref.ly/John10:12) · [ACT 11:6](https://ref.ly/John11:6) · [ROM 1:23](https://ref.ly/Acts1:23) · [JAS 3:7](https://ref.ly/Heb3:7)

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 10:12; Acts 11:6; Romans 1:23; James 3:7


## ἐρυθρός (id: 002079000000000)

ἐρυθρός
=======

ἐρυθρός (G2063)

adj

79\.30 · Features of Objects · Color

red In a number of languages it may be necessary to use a term in [HEB 11:29](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:29) which means ‘reddish,’ since an expression meaning ‘bright red’ might seem entirely inappropriate in speaking of a body of water.

[ACT 7:36](https://ref.ly/John7:36) · [HEB 11:29](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:29)

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 7:36; Hebrews 11:29


## ἔρχομαι (id: 002080000000000)

ἔρχομαι
=======

ἔρχομαι (G2064)

ἀνέρχομαι · ἀντιπαρέρχομαι · ἀπέρχομαι · διέρχομαι · εἰσέρχομαι · ἔλευσις · ἐξέρχομαι · ἐπανέρχομαι · ἐπεισέρχομαι · ἐπέρχομαι · κατέρχομαι · παρεισέρχομαι · παρέρχομαι · περιέρχομαι · προέρχομαι · προσέρχομαι · συνεισέρχομαι · συνέρχομαι

15\.7 · Linear Movement · Move, Come/Go

to come, to go to move from one place to another, either coming or going1 Note that in [MRK 4:21](https://ref.ly/Matt4:21) the subject of ἔρχεται is not something that would normally move by its own force but would need to be brought. Accordingly, in a number of languages it is necessary to say ‘no one brings a lamp ...’

89\.43 · Relations · Result

**Collocation:** ἔρχομαι εἰς

to result in, to end up being (an idiom, literally: to come into) to result in a state

[ACT 19:27](https://ref.ly/John19:27) · [2CO 12:1](https://ref.ly/1Cor12:1) · [1TI 2:4](https://ref.ly/2Thess2:4)

88\.233 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Hypocrisy, Pretense

**Collocation:** ἔρχομαι ἐν ἐνδύμασιν προβάτων

to pretend to be good, to act hypocritically, to come in sheeps clothing (an idiom, literally: to come in sheeps clothing) to pretend to be harmless when in reality one is dangerous and destructive In many languages it is possible to retain the idiom ἔρχομαι ἐν ἐνδύμασιν προβάτων, since the context seems to explain rather clearly what is meant. However, in a number of languages it may be necessary to translate ἔρχομαι ἐν ἐνδύμασιν προβάτων as ‘to come looking like sheep’ or ‘to come looking as innocent as sheep.’

[MAT 7:15](https://ref.ly/Matt7:15)

1 ἔρχομαι\[a] involves a highly generic meaning of movement from one place to another, either coming or going. In this sense ἔρχομαι\[a] contrasts with the meaning of ἔρχομαι\[b] to come ([15\.81](#15.81)), which in turn contrasts in certain contexts with ὑπάγω\[b] to go away from ([15\.35](#15.35)). In other words, ἔρχομαι occurs on two taxonomic levels in the domain of Linear Movement: (1\) generic, including both coming and going, and (2\) to come, in contrast with to go. This type of analysis seems far more legitimate than trying to set up highly complex series of shifts in so\-called perspective viewpoint. See also footnote b. 2 ἔρχομαι\[b] to come or to come to is a more restricted or specific meaning than ἔρχομαι\[a], which means either to come or to go, that is to say, simply to move from one place to another without specifying any viewpoint character or place (see [15\.7](#15.7)). See also footnote a.

## ἐρωτάω (id: 002081000000000)

ἐρωτάω
======

ἐρωτάω (G2065)

ἀνεξεραύνητος · διερωτάω · ἐξεραυνάω · ἐπερωτάω · ἐπερώτημα

33\.180 · Communication · Question, Answer

to ask, to ask a question to ask for information1 In a number of languages a term such as ἐρωτάω\[a] or ἐπερωτάω\[a] implies an introduction of direct discourse, so that in [LUK 9:45](https://ref.ly/Mark9:45), for example, it may be necessary to translate ‘they were afraid to ask him, What do you mean by what you said?’

[MAT 16:13](https://ref.ly/Matt16:13) · [MAT 19:17](https://ref.ly/Matt19:17) · [MAT 21:24](https://ref.ly/Matt21:24) · [MRK 4:10](https://ref.ly/Matt4:10) · [MRK 8:5](https://ref.ly/Matt8:5) · [LUK 9:45](https://ref.ly/Mark9:45) · [LUK 19:31](https://ref.ly/Mark19:31) · [LUK 20:3](https://ref.ly/Mark20:3) · [LUK 22:68](https://ref.ly/Mark22:68) · [LUK 23:3](https://ref.ly/Mark23:3) · [JHN 1:19](https://ref.ly/Luke1:19) · [JHN 1:21](https://ref.ly/Luke1:21) · [JHN 1:25](https://ref.ly/Luke1:25) · [JHN 5:12](https://ref.ly/Luke5:12) · [JHN 8:7](https://ref.ly/Luke8:7) · [JHN 9:2](https://ref.ly/Luke9:2) · [JHN 9:15](https://ref.ly/Luke9:15) · [JHN 9:19](https://ref.ly/Luke9:19) · [JHN 9:21](https://ref.ly/Luke9:21) · [JHN 16:5](https://ref.ly/Luke16:5) · [JHN 16:19](https://ref.ly/Luke16:19) · [JHN 16:30](https://ref.ly/Luke16:30) · [JHN 18:19](https://ref.ly/Luke18:19) · [JHN 18:21](https://ref.ly/Luke18:21) · [JHN 18:21](https://ref.ly/Luke18:21) · [ACT 1:6](https://ref.ly/John1:6)

33\.161 · Communication · Ask For, Request

to ask for, to request to ask for, usually with the implication of an underlying question2

[MAT 15:23](https://ref.ly/Matt15:23) · [MRK 7:26](https://ref.ly/Matt7:26) · [LUK 4:38](https://ref.ly/Mark4:38) · [LUK 5:3](https://ref.ly/Mark5:3) · [LUK 7:3](https://ref.ly/Mark7:3) · [LUK 7:36](https://ref.ly/Mark7:36) · [LUK 8:37](https://ref.ly/Mark8:37) · [LUK 11:37](https://ref.ly/Mark11:37) · [LUK 14:18](https://ref.ly/Mark14:18) · [LUK 14:19](https://ref.ly/Mark14:19) · [LUK 14:32](https://ref.ly/Mark14:32) · [LUK 16:27](https://ref.ly/Mark16:27) · [JHN 4:31](https://ref.ly/Luke4:31) · [JHN 4:40](https://ref.ly/Luke4:40) · [JHN 4:47](https://ref.ly/Luke4:47) · [JHN 12:21](https://ref.ly/Luke12:21) · [JHN 14:16](https://ref.ly/Luke14:16) · [JHN 16:23](https://ref.ly/Luke16:23) · [JHN 16:26](https://ref.ly/Luke16:26) · [JHN 17:9](https://ref.ly/Luke17:9) · [JHN 17:9](https://ref.ly/Luke17:9) · [JHN 17:15](https://ref.ly/Luke17:15) · [JHN 17:20](https://ref.ly/Luke17:20) · [JHN 19:31](https://ref.ly/Luke19:31) · [JHN 19:38](https://ref.ly/Luke19:38) · [ACT 3:3](https://ref.ly/John3:3) · [ACT 10:48](https://ref.ly/John10:48) · [ACT 16:39](https://ref.ly/John16:39) · [ACT 18:20](https://ref.ly/John18:20) · [ACT 23:18](https://ref.ly/John23:18) · [ACT 23:20](https://ref.ly/John23:20) · [PHP 4:3](https://ref.ly/Eph4:3) · [1TH 4:1](https://ref.ly/Col4:1) · [1TH 5:12](https://ref.ly/Col5:12) · [2TH 2:1](https://ref.ly/1Thess2:1) · [1JN 5:16](https://ref.ly/2Pet5:16) · [2JN 1:5](https://ref.ly/1John1:5)

1 There may be some slight difference of meaning between ἐρωτάω\[a] and ἐπερωτάω\[a], but this cannot be determined from existing contexts. 2 ἐπερωτάω\[b] may be somewhat more intensive in meaning than ἐρωτάω\[b], but both seem to be somewhat weaker in urgency than αἰτέω and παραιτέομαι\[a] ([33\.163](#33.163)).

## ἐσθίω (id: 002083000000000)

ἐσθίω
=====

ἐσθίω (G5315)

κατεσθίω · συνεσθίω

23\.1 · Physiological Processes and States · Eat, Drink

to eat, to drink, to consume food, to use food to consume food, usually solids, but also liquids1 As noted in the contexts of [MAT 12:1](https://ref.ly/Matt12:1) and [1CO 9:7](https://ref.ly/Rom9:7), ἐσθίω\[a] is generic in the sense that it includes the consumption of both solid foods and liquids, but ἐσθίω is not employed in referring to the drinking of alcoholic beverages (see μεθύω\[b] to drink freely of wine or beer, [23\.37](#23.37)).\|In some languages an important distinction is made in terms for ‘eating’ depending upon whether the eating is done by an adult or by a very small child, who in most instances must be helped to eat. Other distinctions in terminology for eating may involve the quantities of food (whether small or extensive), the rapidity with which one eats (gulping or mincing), the state of what is eaten (raw, fresh, cooked) or the type of food, for example, meat, cereal, fruit, or leafy vegetable.

**Collocation:** τὸν ἑαυτοῦ ἄρτον ἐσθίω

[2TH 3:12](https://ref.ly/1Thess3:12)

1 In some languages a very fundamental distinction is made between terms referring to the consumption of solid foods in contrast with liquids (either beverages or soups). For the most part, however, a term meaning ‘eat’ is more generic than one meaning ‘drink,’ since the former is frequently employed to describe the consumption of any type of nourishment, while a term meaning ‘drink’ refers only to beverages or very thin soups or broths.
Still further important distinctions are made in some languages between the consumption of so\-called ‘cold foods’ and ‘hot foods.’ This distinction is not, however, based upon the temperature of the food itself but upon the physiological effect which such foods are supposed to have upon the body in causing coolness or heat. A further important distinction may be made with respect to terms referring to animals eating in contrast with humans eating.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 6:25; Matthew 6:31; Matthew 9:11; Matthew 11:18; Matthew 11:19; Matthew 12:1; Matthew 12:4; Matthew 14:16; Matthew 14:20; Matthew 14:21; Matthew 15:2; Matthew 15:20; Matthew 15:27; Matthew 15:32; Matthew 15:37; Matthew 15:38; Matthew 24:49; Matthew 25:35; Matthew 25:42; Matthew 26:17; Matthew 26:21; Matthew 26:26; Mark 1:6; Mark 2:16; Mark 2:26; Mark 3:20; Mark 5:43; Mark 6:31; Mark 6:36; Mark 6:37; Mark 6:42; Mark 6:44; Mark 7:2; Mark 7:3; Mark 7:4; Mark 7:5; Mark 7:28; Mark 8:1; Mark 8:2; Mark 8:8; Mark 11:14; Mark 14:12; Mark 14:14; Mark 14:18; Mark 14:22; Luke 4:2; Luke 5:30; Luke 5:33; Luke 6:1; Luke 6:4; Luke 7:33; Luke 7:34; Luke 7:36; Luke 8:55; Luke 9:13; Luke 9:17; Luke 10:7; Luke 10:8; Luke 12:19; Luke 12:22; Luke 12:29; Luke 12:45; Luke 13:26; Luke 14:1; Luke 14:15; Luke 15:16; Luke 15:23; Luke 17:8; Luke 17:27; Luke 17:28; Luke 22:8; Luke 22:11; Luke 22:15; Luke 22:16; Luke 22:30; Luke 24:43; John 4:31; John 4:32; John 4:33; John 6:5; John 6:23; John 6:26; John 6:31; John 6:49; John 6:50; John 6:51; John 6:52; John 6:53; John 6:58; John 18:28; Acts 9:9; Acts 10:13; Acts 10:14; Acts 11:7; Acts 23:12; Acts 23:21; Acts 27:35; Romans 14:2; Romans 14:3; Romans 14:6; Romans 14:20; Romans 14:21; Romans 14:23; 1 Corinthians 8:7; 1 Corinthians 8:8; 1 Corinthians 8:10; 1 Corinthians 8:13; 1 Corinthians 9:4; 1 Corinthians 9:7; 1 Corinthians 9:13; 1 Corinthians 10:3; 1 Corinthians 10:7; 1 Corinthians 10:18; 1 Corinthians 10:25; 1 Corinthians 10:27; 1 Corinthians 10:28; 1 Corinthians 10:31; 1 Corinthians 11:20; 1 Corinthians 11:21; 1 Corinthians 11:22; 1 Corinthians 11:26; 1 Corinthians 11:27; 1 Corinthians 11:28; 1 Corinthians 11:29; 1 Corinthians 11:33; 1 Corinthians 11:34; 1 Corinthians 15:32; 2 Thessalonians 3:8; 2 Thessalonians 3:10; 2 Thessalonians 3:12; Hebrews 13:10; Revelation 2:7; Revelation 2:14; Revelation 2:20; Revelation 10:10; Revelation 17:16; Revelation 19:18; Hebrews 10:27; James 5:3

## ἔσω (id: 002090000000000)

ἔσω
===

ἔσω (G2080)

prep · εἰς

83\.13 · Spacial Positions · Among, Between, In, Inside

inside, within, in a position defined as being within certain limits1

[MAT 26:58](https://ref.ly/Matt26:58) · [MRK 14:54](https://ref.ly/Matt14:54) · [MRK 15:16](https://ref.ly/Matt15:16) · [JHN 20:26](https://ref.ly/Luke20:26) · [ACT 5:23](https://ref.ly/John5:23)

11\.11 · Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes · General

insider (a figurative extension of meaning of ἔσω ‘inside,’ [83\.13](#83.13)) a person belonging to a so\-called in\-group (in other words, not being a member of the out\-group in question) See also [11\.10](#11.10).\|In the rendering of [1CO 5:12](https://ref.ly/Rom5:12) it may be necessary to be quite specific about those who constitute outsiders and insiders, for example, ‘why should I judge those who are not believers? should you not judge the believers?’ Note the rendering of TEV for [1CO 5:12](https://ref.ly/Rom5:12): “After all, it is none of my business to judge outsiders. God will judge them. But should you not judge the members of your own fellowship? As the scripture says, ‘Remove the evil man from your group.’ ”

[1CO 5:12](https://ref.ly/Rom5:12)

26\.1 · Psychological Faculties

**Collocation:** ὁ ἔσω (ἄνθρωπος)

the inner being, the inmost being, inwardly (an idiom, literally: the inner (person)) the psychological faculty, including intellectual, emotional, and spiritual aspects, in contrast with the purely physical aspects of human existence In a number of languages it is quite impossible to speak of the psychological faculty of a person as being merely ‘that which is within.’ It is frequently necessary to refer to a particular organ of the body, for example, ‘the heart’ or ‘the liver’ or ‘the spleen.’ In some languages, however, one may refer to this faculty as being ‘the person who stands inside’ or ‘the real person’ or even ‘the other person.’\|In [LUK 17:21](https://ref.ly/Mark17:21) the phrase ἐντὸς ὑμῶν in the statement ἰδοὺ γὰρ ἡ βασιλεία τοῦ θεοῦ ἐντὸς ὑμῶν ἐστιν ‘look, God’s reign is within you’ may constitute a reference to the same inner being designated by the phrases ὁ ἔσω and ὁ ἐν τῷ κρυπτῷ. On this basis some scholars have suggested that the phrase ἐντὸς ὑμῶν can be interpreted as a potentiality for participation and hence be translated ‘within your grasp,’ but it is more likely that one should understand the phrase ἐντὸς ὑμῶν in [LUK 17:21](https://ref.ly/Mark17:21) as a spacial relationship, for example, ‘in your midst’ or ‘among you’ (see [83\.9](#83.9)).

[ROM 7:22](https://ref.ly/Acts7:22) · [2CO 4:16](https://ref.ly/1Cor4:16) · [EPH 3:16](https://ref.ly/Gal3:16)

1 In contexts where an accompanying expression designates movement into an area, ἐν\[a] and εἰς\[d] may be rendered as ‘into.’

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 26:58; Mark 14:54; Mark 15:16; John 20:26; Acts 5:23; 1 Corinthians 5:12; Romans 7:22; 2 Corinthians 4:16; Ephesians 3:16


## ἔσωθεν (id: 002091000000000)

ἔσωθεν
======

ἔσωθεν (G2081)

adv · εἰς

84\.14 · Spacial Extensions · Extension From a Source

from inside extension from a source which is inside

[MRK 7:21](https://ref.ly/Matt7:21) · [LUK 11:7](https://ref.ly/Mark11:7)

83\.16 · Spacial Positions · Among, Between, In, Inside

within, on the inside the inner surface of an object

[MAT 23:25](https://ref.ly/Matt23:25) · [MAT 23:27](https://ref.ly/Matt23:27) · [MAT 23:28](https://ref.ly/Matt23:28) · [LUK 11:40](https://ref.ly/Mark11:40) · [2CO 7:5](https://ref.ly/1Cor7:5) · [REV 4:8](https://ref.ly/Jude4:8) · [REV 5:1](https://ref.ly/Jude5:1)

26\.2 · Psychological Faculties

a person’s inner self, the inner being, within oneself (a figurative extension of meaning of ἔσωθεν\[a] ‘from inside,’ [84\.14](#84.14)) the inner being of a person as the source or agent of thought or behavior A literal translation of ἔσωθεν δέ εἰσιν λύκοι ἅρπαγες in [MAT 7:15](https://ref.ly/Matt7:15) as ‘on the inside they are ravaging wolves’ could be seriously misleading, since in some cultures certain people are regarded as actually being animals but disguised as human beings. It may therefore be necessary to make some adjustments in this passage and to translate as ‘but what they want to do makes them like wild wolves’ or ‘but their minds are like the minds of wild wolves.’ On the other hand, one could employ a strictly literal translation and then have a footnote indicating that this statement in the Scriptures does not confirm local beliefs concerning animals disguised as humans.

[MAT 7:15](https://ref.ly/Matt7:15) · [MRK 7:23](https://ref.ly/Matt7:23) · [LUK 11:39](https://ref.ly/Mark11:39)

* **Associated Passages:** Mark 7:21; Luke 11:7; Matthew 23:25; Matthew 23:27; Matthew 23:28; Luke 11:40; 2 Corinthians 7:5; Revelation 4:8; Revelation 5:1; Matthew 7:15; Mark 7:23; Luke 11:39

67\.208 · Time · Units of Time with Reference to Other Units or Points of Time: Yesterday, Today, Next Day

**Collocation:** τῇ ἑτέρᾳ

the next day, on the next day a day immediately following a previous day2

[ACT 20:15](https://ref.ly/John20:15)

1 Some persons have assumed that the distinction between ἄλλος and ἕτερος is simply a difference between what is another in a series and that which is significantly different in kind, but such a distinction cannot be confirmed from existing contexts, though there may be this type of distinction implied in [GAL 1:6](https://ref.ly/2Cor1:6), [GAL 1:7](https://ref.ly/2Cor1:7) in which another kind of gospel is rejected on the basis that there is no other gospel. Note, however, that both ἄλλος and ἕτερος also occur in the meaning of another, which is not the same (see [58\.37](#58.37)). 2 Rather than treat these phrases as lexical units, one can interpret them as elliptical expressions with the omission of the dative of ἡμέρα\[a] day ([67\.178](#67.178)).

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 11:3; Mark 16:12; Luke 9:29; Acts 17:21; Romans 7:23; 1 Corinthians 15:40; 2 Corinthians 11:4; Galatians 1:6; Hebrews 7:11; Hebrews 7:13; Hebrews 7:15; James 2:25; Jude 1:7; Matthew 6:24; Matthew 8:21; Matthew 10:23; Matthew 11:16; Matthew 12:45; Matthew 15:30; Matthew 16:14; Luke 3:18; Luke 4:43; Luke 5:7; Luke 6:6; Luke 7:41; Luke 8:3; Luke 8:6; Luke 8:7; Luke 8:8; Luke 9:56; Luke 9:59; Luke 9:61; Luke 10:1; Luke 11:16; Luke 11:26; Luke 14:19; Luke 14:20; Luke 14:31; Luke 16:7; Luke 16:13; Luke 16:18; Luke 17:34; Luke 17:35; Luke 18:10; Luke 19:20; Luke 20:11; Luke 22:58; Luke 22:65; Luke 23:32; Luke 23:40; John 19:37; Acts 1:20; Acts 2:4; Acts 2:13; Acts 2:40; Acts 4:12; Acts 7:18; Acts 8:34; Acts 12:17; Acts 13:35; Acts 15:35; Acts 17:7; Acts 17:34; Acts 20:15; Acts 23:6; Acts 27:1; Acts 27:3; Romans 2:1; Romans 2:21; Romans 7:3; Romans 7:4; Romans 8:39; Romans 13:8; Romans 13:9; 1 Corinthians 3:4; 1 Corinthians 4:6; 1 Corinthians 6:1; 1 Corinthians 10:24; 1 Corinthians 10:29; 1 Corinthians 12:9; 1 Corinthians 12:10; 1 Corinthians 14:17; 1 Corinthians 14:21; 2 Corinthians 8:8; Galatians 1:19; Galatians 6:4; Ephesians 3:5; Philippians 2:4; 1 Timothy 1:10; 2 Timothy 2:2; Hebrews 5:6; Hebrews 11:36

## ἔτος (id: 002104000000000)

ἔτος
====

ἔτος (G2094)

noun · διετία · ἑκατονταετής · τεσσερακονταετής · τριετία

67\.167 · Time · Definite Units of Time: Year, Month, Week, Day, Hour

year a unit of time involving a complete cycle of seasons In a number of languages, years are described in terms of repeated seasons. For example, some languages would speak of ‘twelve years’ as being ‘twelve winters’ or ‘twelve summers,’ while in other languages one might have an expression such as ‘twelve rainy seasons’ or ‘twelve returns of the sun.’

67\.94 · Time · Duration of Time without Reference to Points or Units of Time: Time, Spend Time, Always, Eternal, Old, Immediately, Young

**Collocation:** ἔτη τεσσεράκοντα

a very long time possibly a symbolic temporal expression denoting a very long period of time Though most translations render ἔτη τεσσεράκοντα as ‘forty years,’ the expression probably is to be understood as meaning an unusually long period of time. See also discussion at [67\.93](#67.93).

[ACT 7:30](https://ref.ly/John7:30) · [ACT 7:36](https://ref.ly/John7:36) · [ACT 7:42](https://ref.ly/John7:42) · [ACT 13:21](https://ref.ly/John13:21)

67\.60 · Time · A Point of Time with Reference to Other Points of Time: Before, Long Ago, Now, At the Same Time, When, About, After

**Collocation:** δι᾽ ἐτῶν

some years later (an idiom, literally: through years) a point of time subsequent to another point of time after an interval of some years

## εὐδία (id: 002116000000000)

εὐδία
=====

εὐδία (G2105)

noun

14\.1 · Physical Events and States · Weather

fair weather sunny, mild weather without strong winds Fair weather may be referred to by a number of different expressions, for example, ‘growing weather’ or ‘cloudless skies’ or ‘happy weather.’ The variety of expressions depends largely upon the type of activity associated with weather or the atmospheric conditions typical of what people regard as fair weather. In a number of languages fair weather is expressed as a negative of bad weather, that is to say, ‘a not stormy day’ or ‘a not windy day.’

[MAT 16:2](https://ref.ly/Matt16:2)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 16:2

25\.113 · Attitudes and Emotions · Enjoy, Take Pleasure In, Be Fond of Doing

to enjoy to take pleasure in something in that it fulfills one’s desires In some languages the equivalent of ‘to enjoy’ is ‘to make someone happy’ or ‘to make one feel good.’ This expression in [2TH 2:12](https://ref.ly/1Thess2:12) may be rendered as ‘but when they are sinning, they feel good’ or ‘but to sin makes them happy.’

## εὐθέως (id: 002123000000000)

εὐθέως
======

εὐθέως (G2112)

adv · εὐθύς

67\.53 · Time · A Point of Time with Reference to Other Points of Time: Before, Long Ago, Now, At the Same Time, When, About, After

immediately, right away, then a point of time immediately subsequent to a previous point of time (the actual interval of time differs appreciably, depending upon the nature of the events and the manner in which the sequence is interpreted by the writer) It is impossible to determine from the context whether εὐθέως in [3JN 1:14](https://ref.ly/2John1:14) should be understood as referring to a very short lapse of time and therefore be rendered as ‘very soon,’ or whether it refers to the very next event which is relevant to the total context.

## εὐθύνω (id: 002128000000000)

εὐθύνω
======

εὐθύνω (G2116)

θύνω · εὐθύς

79\.89 · Features of Objects · Straight, Crooked

to cause to be straight, to make straight, to straighten to make something straight In a number of languages a distinction is made between making some object straight and straightening out a road. The first meaning may be equivalent to ‘bending something straight,’ while the latter meaning may be literally ‘to avoid the curves.’

[JHN 1:23](https://ref.ly/Luke1:23)

54\.21 · Maritime Activities

to pilot a ship, to steer a course to steer a ship on its course In a number of languages the equivalent of ‘piloting a ship’ is ‘to hold the rudder’ or ‘to hold the wheel’ (referring to the wheel which controls the position of the rudder).

[JAS 3:4](https://ref.ly/Heb3:4)

* **Associated Passages:** John 1:23; James 3:4


## εὐθύς (id: 002129000000000)

εὐθύς
=====

εὐθύς (G2117a)

adj, adv

79\.88 · Features of Objects · Straight, Crooked

straight, direct pertaining to being straight in contrast to what is crooked1

[MAT 3:3](https://ref.ly/Matt3:3) · [MRK 1:3](https://ref.ly/Matt1:3) · [LUK 3:4](https://ref.ly/Mark3:4) · [LUK 3:5](https://ref.ly/Mark3:5) · [ACT 9:11](https://ref.ly/John9:11) · [ACT 13:10](https://ref.ly/John13:10) · [2PE 2:15](https://ref.ly/1Pet2:15)

88\.17 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Just, Righteous

just, right, upright pertaining to being just and right

[ACT 8:21](https://ref.ly/John8:21)

88\.18 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Just, Righteous

**Collocation:** εὐθεῖα ὁδός

right way, correct manner of life, just way of life (an idiom, literally: a straight road) the right or correct pattern of behavior

[ACT 13:10](https://ref.ly/John13:10) · [2PE 2:15](https://ref.ly/1Pet2:15)

adv

67\.53 · Time · A Point of Time with Reference to Other Points of Time: Before, Long Ago, Now, At the Same Time, When, About, After

immediately, right away, then a point of time immediately subsequent to a previous point of time (the actual interval of time differs appreciably, depending upon the nature of the events and the manner in which the sequence is interpreted by the writer) In [MRK 1:21](https://ref.ly/Matt1:21) εὐθύς probably implies what was done on the immediately following Sabbath. Accordingly, one may translate this expression in [MRK 1:21](https://ref.ly/Matt1:21) as ‘and on the next Sabbath he went into the synagogue and taught.’

[MAT 3:16](https://ref.ly/Matt3:16) · [MAT 13:20](https://ref.ly/Matt13:20) · [MAT 13:21](https://ref.ly/Matt13:21) · [MAT 14:27](https://ref.ly/Matt14:27) · [MAT 21:3](https://ref.ly/Matt21:3) · [MRK 1:10](https://ref.ly/Matt1:10) · [MRK 1:12](https://ref.ly/Matt1:12) · [MRK 1:18](https://ref.ly/Matt1:18) · [MRK 1:20](https://ref.ly/Matt1:20) · [MRK 1:21](https://ref.ly/Matt1:21) · [MRK 1:23](https://ref.ly/Matt1:23) · [MRK 1:28](https://ref.ly/Matt1:28) · [MRK 1:29](https://ref.ly/Matt1:29) · [MRK 1:30](https://ref.ly/Matt1:30) · [MRK 1:42](https://ref.ly/Matt1:42) · [MRK 1:43](https://ref.ly/Matt1:43) · [MRK 2:8](https://ref.ly/Matt2:8) · [MRK 2:12](https://ref.ly/Matt2:12) · [MRK 3:6](https://ref.ly/Matt3:6) · [MRK 4:5](https://ref.ly/Matt4:5) · [MRK 4:15](https://ref.ly/Matt4:15) · [MRK 4:16](https://ref.ly/Matt4:16) · [MRK 4:17](https://ref.ly/Matt4:17) · [MRK 4:29](https://ref.ly/Matt4:29) · [MRK 5:2](https://ref.ly/Matt5:2) · [MRK 5:29](https://ref.ly/Matt5:29) · [MRK 5:30](https://ref.ly/Matt5:30) · [MRK 5:42](https://ref.ly/Matt5:42) · [MRK 5:42](https://ref.ly/Matt5:42) · [MRK 6:25](https://ref.ly/Matt6:25) · [MRK 6:27](https://ref.ly/Matt6:27) · [MRK 6:45](https://ref.ly/Matt6:45) · [MRK 6:50](https://ref.ly/Matt6:50) · [MRK 6:54](https://ref.ly/Matt6:54) · [MRK 7:25](https://ref.ly/Matt7:25) · [MRK 8:10](https://ref.ly/Matt8:10) · [MRK 9:15](https://ref.ly/Matt9:15) · [MRK 9:20](https://ref.ly/Matt9:20) · [MRK 9:24](https://ref.ly/Matt9:24) · [MRK 10:52](https://ref.ly/Matt10:52) · [MRK 11:2](https://ref.ly/Matt11:2) · [MRK 11:3](https://ref.ly/Matt11:3) · [MRK 14:43](https://ref.ly/Matt14:43) · [MRK 14:45](https://ref.ly/Matt14:45) · [MRK 14:72](https://ref.ly/Matt14:72) · [MRK 15:1](https://ref.ly/Matt15:1) · [LUK 6:49](https://ref.ly/Mark6:49) · [JHN 13:30](https://ref.ly/Luke13:30) · [JHN 13:32](https://ref.ly/Luke13:32) · [JHN 19:34](https://ref.ly/Luke19:34) · [ACT 10:16](https://ref.ly/John10:16)

## εὔκοπος (id: 002135000000000)

εὔκοπος
=======

εὔκοπος (G2123)

adj · κόπτω

22\.39 · Trouble, Hardship, Relief, Favorable Circumstances · Easy, Light

easy, without trouble (occurring only in the comparative form in the NT) pertaining to that which is easy, in the sense of not requiring great effort or work In some languages it is difficult to render in a succinct manner an expression such as ‘easier to do,’ for a comparative expression involving degree of effort may require complex restructuring. Accordingly, one can often translate the first part of this expression in [MAT 19:24](https://ref.ly/Matt19:24) as ‘a camel does not have to struggle as much to go through the eye of a needle as ...’

[MAT 9:5](https://ref.ly/Matt9:5) · [MAT 19:24](https://ref.ly/Matt19:24) · [MRK 2:9](https://ref.ly/Matt2:9) · [MRK 10:25](https://ref.ly/Matt10:25) · [LUK 5:23](https://ref.ly/Mark5:23) · [LUK 16:17](https://ref.ly/Mark16:17) · [LUK 18:25](https://ref.ly/Mark18:25)

## εὐλάβεια (id: 002136000000000)

εὐλάβεια
========

εὐλάβεια (G2124)

noun · λαμβάνω

53\.7 · Religious Activities · Religious Practice

to reverence, reverent regard for, reverence to show reverent regard for In εὐλαβέομαι\[a] and εὐλάβεια there is also a certain element of awe, which may be interpreted in some instances as implying even fear. The implication of such reverent fear or awe is, of course, obedience, and some scholars prefer to interpret these terms in [HEB 11:7](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:7) and [HEB 5:7](https://ref.ly/Phlm5:7) as meaning ‘to obey’ (see [36\.13](#36.13)) or ‘obedience.’

[HEB 5:7](https://ref.ly/Phlm5:7) · [HEB 12:28](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:28)

* **Associated Passages:** Hebrews 5:7; Hebrews 12:28

==========

to reverence, reverent regard for, reverence to show reverent regard for In εὐλαβέομαι\[a] and εὐλάβεια there is also a certain element of awe, which may be interpreted in some instances as implying even fear. The implication of such reverent fear or awe is, of course, obedience, and some scholars prefer to interpret these terms in [HEB 11:7](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:7) and [HEB 5:7](https://ref.ly/Phlm5:7) as meaning ‘to obey’ (see [36\.13](#36.13)) or ‘obedience.’

[HEB 11:7](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:7)

=======

λέγω

33\.356 · Communication · Praise

to praise, to speak well of to speak of something in favorable terms

[MAT 14:19](https://ref.ly/Matt14:19) · [MAT 26:26](https://ref.ly/Matt26:26) · [MRK 6:41](https://ref.ly/Matt6:41) · [MRK 8:7](https://ref.ly/Matt8:7) · [MRK 14:22](https://ref.ly/Matt14:22) · [LUK 1:64](https://ref.ly/Mark1:64) · [LUK 2:28](https://ref.ly/Mark2:28) · [LUK 9:16](https://ref.ly/Mark9:16) · [LUK 24:30](https://ref.ly/Mark24:30) · [LUK 24:53](https://ref.ly/Mark24:53) · [1CO 10:16](https://ref.ly/Rom10:16) · [1CO 14:16](https://ref.ly/Rom14:16) · [JAS 3:9](https://ref.ly/Heb3:9)

33\.470 · Communication · Bless, Curse

to bless, blessing to ask God to bestow divine favor on, with the implication that the verbal act itself constitutes a significant benefit1 In a number of languages the closest equivalent of ‘to bless’ is ‘to pray God on behalf of’ or ‘to ask God to do something good for.’

[MAT 21:9](https://ref.ly/Matt21:9) · [MAT 23:39](https://ref.ly/Matt23:39) · [MRK 11:9](https://ref.ly/Matt11:9) · [MRK 11:10](https://ref.ly/Matt11:10) · [LUK 2:34](https://ref.ly/Mark2:34) · [LUK 6:28](https://ref.ly/Mark6:28) · [LUK 13:35](https://ref.ly/Mark13:35) · [LUK 19:38](https://ref.ly/Mark19:38) · [LUK 24:50](https://ref.ly/Mark24:50) · [LUK 24:51](https://ref.ly/Mark24:51) · [JHN 12:13](https://ref.ly/Luke12:13) · [ROM 12:14](https://ref.ly/Acts12:14) · [ROM 12:14](https://ref.ly/Acts12:14) · [1CO 4:12](https://ref.ly/Rom4:12) · [HEB 7:1](https://ref.ly/Phlm7:1) · [HEB 7:6](https://ref.ly/Phlm7:6) · [HEB 7:7](https://ref.ly/Phlm7:7) · [HEB 11:20](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:20) · [HEB 11:21](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:21) · [1PE 3:9](https://ref.ly/Jas3:9)

88\.69 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Kindness, Harshness

to act kindly toward, to bless to provide benefits, often with the implication of certain supernatural factors involved In [EPH 1:3](https://ref.ly/Gal1:3) there is an obvious play on the words εὐλογητός and εὐλογήσας. The term εὐλογητός designates ‘the one to be praised’ (see [33\.362](#33.362)), but εὐλογήσας designates ‘blessing.’

[MAT 25:34](https://ref.ly/Matt25:34) · [LUK 1:42](https://ref.ly/Mark1:42) · [LUK 1:42](https://ref.ly/Mark1:42) · [ACT 3:26](https://ref.ly/John3:26) · [GAL 3:9](https://ref.ly/2Cor3:9) · [EPH 1:3](https://ref.ly/Gal1:3) · [HEB 6:14](https://ref.ly/Phlm6:14) · [HEB 6:14](https://ref.ly/Phlm6:14)

1 It is possible that κατευλογέω is somewhat more emphatic than εὐλογέω\[b], but this cannot be determined from existing contexts.

=======

noun · λέγω

33\.366 · Communication · Flatter

flattery, flattering talk excessive praise In some languages flattery is expressed in a descriptive manner, for example, ‘to say that a person is wonderful when he really isn’t.’ Sometimes flattery is expressed idiomatically, for example, ‘to use big words about a small person’ or ‘to call a donkey a man.’

[ROM 16:18](https://ref.ly/Acts16:18)

33\.470 · Communication · Bless, Curse

to bless, blessing to ask God to bestow divine favor on, with the implication that the verbal act itself constitutes a significant benefit In a number of languages the closest equivalent of ‘to bless’ is ‘to pray God on behalf of’ or ‘to ask God to do something good for.’

[1CO 10:16](https://ref.ly/Rom10:16) · [HEB 6:7](https://ref.ly/Phlm6:7) · [JAS 3:10](https://ref.ly/Heb3:10) · [1PE 3:9](https://ref.ly/Jas3:9)

88\.70 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Kindness, Harshness

blessing, benefit (derivative of εὐλογέω\[c] ‘to bless,’ [88\.69](#88.69)) the content of the act of blessing In [ROM 15:29](https://ref.ly/Acts15:29) this blessing is something which Christ has extended to Paul, in other words, a type of special favor granted to Paul by Christ.

57\.105 · Possess, Transfer, Exchange · Give

gift, blessing, contribution (derivative of εὐλογέω\[b] ‘to bless,’ [33\.470](#33.470)) that which is bestowed or given as a blessing or benefit

[2CO 9:5](https://ref.ly/1Cor9:5) · [2CO 9:5](https://ref.ly/1Cor9:5)

59\.56 · Quantity · Abundance, Excess, Sparing

**Collocation:** ἐπ᾽ εὐλογίαις

large amount, abundant amount, abundance (an idiom, literally: on the basis of blessings) a large amount of something, with the implication of blessing or benefit In translating [2CO 9:6](https://ref.ly/1Cor9:6) it may be necessary to indicate clearly the objects which form the abundance, for example, ‘the one who plants a great deal of grain will reap a very large crop.’

[2CO 9:6](https://ref.ly/1Cor9:6) · [2CO 9:6](https://ref.ly/1Cor9:6)

## εὐνοῦχος (id: 002147000000000)

εὐνοῦχος
========

εὐνοῦχος (G2135)

noun

9\.25 · People · Males

eunuch a castrated male person1 In the ancient Middle East eunuchs were frequently in charge of royal harems and sometimes rose to high positions of state. The reference to the eunuch in [ACT 8:34](https://ref.ly/John8:34) is best interpreted as ‘court official’ (see [37\.85](#37.85)).\|Since in many societies castration of human males is not known, any attempt to employ a descriptive explanation within the text might seem unnecessarily crude and even vulgar. A number of translators have therefore preferred to borrow a term for eunuch and then place the explanation of its meaning in a glossary or word list.

[MAT 19:12](https://ref.ly/Matt19:12) · [MAT 19:12](https://ref.ly/Matt19:12) · [ACT 8:27](https://ref.ly/John8:27) · [ACT 8:34](https://ref.ly/John8:34) · [ACT 8:36](https://ref.ly/John8:36) · [ACT 8:38](https://ref.ly/John8:38) · [ACT 8:39](https://ref.ly/John8:39)

9\.28 · People · Males

impotent male a human male who without being castrated is by nature incapable of sexual intercourse1

[MAT 19:12](https://ref.ly/Matt19:12)

9\.29 · People · Males

celibate a male person who abstains from marriage without being necessarily impotent1

[MAT 19:12](https://ref.ly/Matt19:12)

37\.85 · Control, Rule · Rule, Govern

court official, eunuch an official of an Oriental court who was entrusted with various important responsibilities and who was also a eunuch In a context such as [ACT 8:34](https://ref.ly/John8:34), the focus of meaning of εὐνοῦχος\[d] seems to be upon the person’s official responsibilities and position rather than upon his physical condition. It may therefore be more in keeping with the context to employ an expression such as ‘official of the court’ or ‘important official.’ On the other hand, in view of a eunuch’s limited acceptance in Jewish religion, the mention of the physical condition would fit Luke’s theological purposes well.

[ACT 8:27](https://ref.ly/John8:27) · [ACT 8:34](https://ref.ly/John8:34) · [ACT 8:36](https://ref.ly/John8:36) · [ACT 8:38](https://ref.ly/John8:38) · [ACT 8:39](https://ref.ly/John8:39)

1 It would be possible to treat εὐνοῦχος\[a]\[b]\[c] ([9\.25](#9.25), 28, 29\) as identifying various physiological states resulting from the different processes or states referred to by the verb εὐνουχίζω ([9\.26](#9.26) and [9\.27](#9.27)), but since εὐνοῦχος is frequently used absolutely in various senses, these three meanings seem best classified under the subdomain of Males.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 19:12; Acts 8:27; Acts 8:34; Acts 8:36; Acts 8:38; Acts 8:39

## εὐρακύλων (id: 002160000000000)

εὐρακύλων
=========

εὐρακύλων (G2148a) — Εὐρακύλων

noun

14\.9 · Physical Events and States · Wind

**Collocation:** Εὐρακύλων

a Northeaster a hybrid formation from Greek and Latin, meaning literally either ‘east wind’ or ‘broad wind’: a strong storm wind blowing from the northeast It is, of course, possible to use the form Εὐρακύλων as a proper name, but this is probably much less meaningful than employing a term such as ‘strong northeast wind’ or ‘a strong storm coming from the northeast.’ In instances in which languages do not readily and easily distinguish between north and northeast or between east and northeast, it may be satisfactory simply to use a term such as ‘east,’ but frequently one can use a designation for ‘northeast’ such as ‘between the north and the east’ or ‘a little to the north of east.’

[ACT 27:14](https://ref.ly/John27:14)1

1 ACT 27:14 Some manuscripts read Εὐροκλύδων, explained as ‘an east wind that stirs up waves.’ Another form is Εὐρυκλύδων ‘the wind that stirs up broad waves.’ These two forms, however, are regarded by most scholars as being scribal errors

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 27:14

90\.70 · Case · Experiencer

to begin to experience, to come into an experience, to attain to begin to experience an event or state

[MAT 11:29](https://ref.ly/Matt11:29) · [ACT 7:46](https://ref.ly/John7:46) · [ACT 13:22](https://ref.ly/John13:22) · [ROM 4:1](https://ref.ly/Acts4:1) · [2CO 5:3](https://ref.ly/1Cor5:3) · [2TI 1:18](https://ref.ly/1Tim1:18) · [HEB 9:12](https://ref.ly/Phlm9:12)

13\.7 · Be, Become, Exist, Happen · State

to be found to be, to discover to be, to turn out to be to be in a state which has not been anticipated2

[ACT 5:39](https://ref.ly/John5:39) · [ROM 7:10](https://ref.ly/Acts7:10) · [1CO 4:2](https://ref.ly/Rom4:2) · [1CO 15:15](https://ref.ly/Rom15:15) · [2CO 11:12](https://ref.ly/1Cor11:12) · [GAL 2:17](https://ref.ly/2Cor2:17) · [PHP 2:7](https://ref.ly/Eph2:7) · [2PE 3:14](https://ref.ly/1Pet3:14) · [REV 5:4](https://ref.ly/Jude5:4)

1 MAT 10:39 The implication of ‘to try to’ is derived from the form of the present participle. 2 εὑρίσκομαι in the sense of ‘to be found to be’ is typical of a complex semantic structure which involves not only intellectual discovery but a type of semantic equational relation.

## εὐσέβεια (id: 002163000000000)

εὐσέβεια
========

εὐσέβεια (G2150)

noun · σέβομαι

53\.1 · Religious Activities · Religious Practice

religion, piety appropriate beliefs and devout practice of obligations relating to supernatural persons and powers In a number of languages there is no specific term equivalent to ‘religion,’ but one may always speak of this phase of culture by some phrase such as ‘how to act toward God’ or ‘what one does to placate spirits’ or ‘how one worships.’

[1TI 3:16](https://ref.ly/2Thess3:16) · [1TI 6:3](https://ref.ly/2Thess6:3) · [1TI 6:5](https://ref.ly/2Thess6:5) · [1TI 6:6](https://ref.ly/2Thess6:6) · [2TI 3:5](https://ref.ly/1Tim3:5) · [TIT 1:1](https://ref.ly/2Tim1:1)

53\.5 · Religious Activities · Religious Practice

piety, godliness behavior reflecting correct religious beliefs and attitudes In a number of languages εὐσέβεια in [1TI 2:2](https://ref.ly/2Thess2:2) may be appropriately translated as ‘to live as God would have us live’ or ‘to live as God has told us we should live.’

[ACT 3:12](https://ref.ly/John3:12) · [1TI 2:2](https://ref.ly/2Thess2:2) · [1TI 4:7](https://ref.ly/2Thess4:7) · [1TI 4:8](https://ref.ly/2Thess4:8) · [1TI 6:11](https://ref.ly/2Thess6:11) · [2TI 3:5](https://ref.ly/1Tim3:5) · [TIT 1:1](https://ref.ly/2Tim1:1) · [2PE 1:3](https://ref.ly/1Pet1:3) · [2PE 1:6](https://ref.ly/1Pet1:6) · [2PE 1:7](https://ref.ly/1Pet1:7) · [2PE 3:11](https://ref.ly/1Pet3:11)

* **Associated Passages:** 1 Timothy 3:16; 1 Timothy 6:3; 1 Timothy 6:5; 1 Timothy 6:6; 2 Timothy 3:5; Titus 1:1; Acts 3:12; 1 Timothy 2:2; 1 Timothy 4:7; 1 Timothy 4:8; 1 Timothy 6:11; 2 Peter 1:3; 2 Peter 1:6; 2 Peter 1:7; 2 Peter 3:11


## εὐσεβέω (id: 002164000000000)

εὐσεβέω
=======

εὐσεβέω (G2151)

σέβομαι

53\.53 · Religious Activities · Worship, Reverence

to worship, to venerate to express in attitude and ritual one’s allegiance to and regard for deity In a number of languages worship is expressed in an idiomatic manner, for example, ‘to bow down before,’ ‘to lower one’s head before,’ ‘to raise one’s arms to,’ ‘to sing to,’ ‘to honor.’ It is important in selecting an expression for worship to employ a term or phrase which will include various aspects of worship.

[ACT 17:23](https://ref.ly/John17:23)

71\.29 · Mode · Should, Ought

to fulfill one’s duties, to complete one’s religious duty to fulfill one’s socio\-religious obligations

[1TI 5:4](https://ref.ly/2Thess5:4)

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 17:23; 1 Timothy 5:4


## εὐσεβής (id: 002165000000000)

εὐσεβής
=======

εὐσεβής (G2152)

adj · σέβομαι

53\.6 · Religious Activities · Religious Practice

devout, pious, religious pertaining to being devoted to a proper expression of religious beliefs1 In a number of languages the concept of ‘living a godly life’ may be best expressed as ‘to live as God would have one live’ or ‘to live like one should who believes in God’ or ‘to always do what God requires.’ In some languages, however, the only equivalent is ‘to live a good life’ or ‘to live always doing good to others.’ The shift from the focus upon religious belief to good living or being good to others results from the fact that in some religious systems there is little or no connection between religious beliefs and moral behavior.

[ACT 10:2](https://ref.ly/John10:2) · [ACT 10:7](https://ref.ly/John10:7) · [2PE 2:9](https://ref.ly/1Pet2:9)

1 There are no doubt certain subtle differences of meaning in this series, but it is difficult to define these distinctions on the basis of existing contexts.

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 10:2; Acts 10:7; 2 Peter 2:9

=======

devout, pious, religious pertaining to being devoted to a proper expression of religious beliefs1 In a number of languages the concept of ‘living a godly life’ may be best expressed as ‘to live as God would have one live’ or ‘to live like one should who believes in God’ or ‘to always do what God requires.’ In some languages, however, the only equivalent is ‘to live a good life’ or ‘to live always doing good to others.’ The shift from the focus upon religious belief to good living or being good to others results from the fact that in some religious systems there is little or no connection between religious beliefs and moral behavior.

[2TI 3:12](https://ref.ly/1Tim3:12) · [TIT 2:12](https://ref.ly/2Tim2:12)

1 There are no doubt certain subtle differences of meaning in this series, but it is difficult to define these distinctions on the basis of existing contexts.

## εὐχαριστέω (id: 002181000000000)

εὐχαριστέω
==========

εὐχαριστέω (G2168)

χαίρω

33\.349 · Communication · Thanks

to thank, to give thanks to express gratitude for benefits or blessings Thanks is often expressed in highly idiomatic ways. For example, in some languages one says thank you by saying ‘may God pay you.’ Such a phrase may be so standardized as to even be used in expressing thankfulness to God himself. In other instances, thankfulness may be expressed as ‘you have made my heart warm.’

## εὐχαριστία (id: 002182000000000)

εὐχαριστία
==========

εὐχαριστία (G2169)

noun · χαίρω

33\.349 · Communication · Thanks

thanksgiving, thankfulness expressing gratitude for benefits or blessings Thanks is often expressed in highly idiomatic ways. For example, in some languages one says thank you by saying ‘may God pay you.’ Such a phrase may be so standardized as to even be used in expressing thankfulness to God himself. In other instances, thankfulness may be expressed as ‘you have made my heart warm.’

## εὐχάριστος (id: 002183000000000)

εὐχάριστος
==========

εὐχάριστος (G2170)

adj · χαίρω

25\.99 · Attitudes and Emotions · Thankful, Grateful

thankful pertaining to being thankful for what has been done to or for someone Expressions for ‘thankfulness’ are frequently idiomatic, for example, ‘to have a full heart’ or ‘to speak from the heart’ or even ‘to say, You are very kind.’ For another interpretation of εὐχάριστος in [COL 3:15](https://ref.ly/Phil3:15), see [33\.352](#33.352).

[COL 3:15](https://ref.ly/Phil3:15)

33\.352 · Communication · Thanks

thankful, thanking, grateful (derivative of εὐχαριστέω\[a] ‘to thank,’ [33\.349](#33.349)) pertaining to expressing thanks For another interpretation of εὐχάριστος in [COL 3:15](https://ref.ly/Phil3:15), see [25\.99](#25.99).

## εὐχή (id: 002184000000000)

εὐχή
====

εὐχή (G2171)

noun · εὔχομα

33\.178 · Communication · Pray

prayer the act of speaking to or making requests of God1 In some languages there are a number of different terms used for prayer depending upon the nature of the content, for example, requests for material blessing, pleas for spiritual help, intercession for others, thanksgiving, and praise. There may also be important distinctions on the basis of urgency and need. The most generic expression for prayer may simply be ‘to speak to God.’ It is normally best to avoid an expression which means primarily ‘to recite.’

[JAS 5:15](https://ref.ly/Heb5:15)

33\.469 · Communication · Swear, Put Under Oath, Vow

vow a promise to God that one will do something, with the implication that failure to act accordingly will result in divine sanctions against the person in question It may, however, be necessary in some languages to be somewhat more specific in rendering ‘vow,’ so that one may need to translate this expression in [ACT 21:23](https://ref.ly/John21:23) as ‘there are four men here who have promised God that they will do something.’

[ACT 18:18](https://ref.ly/John18:18) · [ACT 21:23](https://ref.ly/John21:23)

1 It is possible that εὔχομαι\[a] and προσεύχομαι differ somewhat in the extent to which focus is upon the person to whom the prayer is addressed, but this cannot be determined from NT usage.

* **Associated Passages:** James 5:15; Acts 18:18; Acts 21:23


## εὔχομαι (id: 002185000000000)

εὔχομαι
=======

εὔχομαι (G2172)

εὐχή · προσεύχομαι · προσευχή

33\.178 · Communication · Pray

to pray, to speak to God, to ask God for, prayer to speak to or to make requests of God1 In some languages there are a number of different terms used for prayer depending upon the nature of the content, for example, requests for material blessing, pleas for spiritual help, intercession for others, thanksgiving, and praise. There may also be important distinctions on the basis of urgency and need. The most generic expression for prayer may simply be ‘to speak to God.’ It is normally best to avoid an expression which means primarily ‘to recite.’

[ACT 26:29](https://ref.ly/John26:29) · [2CO 13:7](https://ref.ly/1Cor13:7) · [2CO 13:9](https://ref.ly/1Cor13:9) · [JAS 5:16](https://ref.ly/Heb5:16) · [3JN 1:2](https://ref.ly/2John1:2)

25\.6 · Attitudes and Emotions · Desire, Want, Wish

to desire, to wish to desire something, with the implication of a pious wish

[ACT 26:29](https://ref.ly/John26:29) · [ACT 27:29](https://ref.ly/John27:29) · [ROM 9:3](https://ref.ly/Acts9:3)2 · [3JN 1:2](https://ref.ly/2John1:2)

1 It is possible that εὔχομαι\[a] and προσεύχομαι differ somewhat in the extent to which focus is upon the person to whom the prayer is addressed, but this cannot be determined from NT usage. 2 ROM 9:3 It is possible, but not very probable, that εὔχομαι in [ROM 9:3](https://ref.ly/Acts9:3) could mean ‘to pray’ (see [33\.178](#33.178)).

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 26:29; 2 Corinthians 13:7; 2 Corinthians 13:9; James 5:16; 3 John 1:2; Acts 27:29; Romans 9:3

εὐωδία
======

noun · ὄζω

79\.46 · Features of Objects · Fragrance, Odor

aroma, fragrance a pleasant or sweet\-smelling odor Since the reference in [PHP 4:18](https://ref.ly/Eph4:18) is to a sacrifice, the use of εὐωδία may seem strange, if not contradictory, in many languages, since the odor from burning flesh is normally not regarded as being fragrant or pleasant. It may therefore be necessary to include a marginal note to explain this usage.

## ἐφημερία (id: 002195000000000)

ἐφημερία
========

ἐφημερία (G2183)

noun · ἡμέρα

11\.47 · Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes · Socio\-Religious

work group, division a class or division of persons who perform certain daily duties (in the one NT occurrence, a reference to a group of priests performing certain functions in the Temple at Jerusalem) It may be necessary to expand somewhat the expression ‘the work group of Abijah’ ([LUK 1:5](https://ref.ly/Mark1:5)) to read ‘a work group known as the division of Abijah.’ Otherwise, one might get the impression that this was a work group under the command of Abijah or a group of persons who belonged specifically to the person named Abijah. If it is difficult to obtain a satisfactory expression for ‘work group,’ one can of course translate the final clause of [LUK 1:5](https://ref.ly/Mark1:5) as simply ‘who belonged to a group of priests known as the group of Abijah.’

[LUK 1:5](https://ref.ly/Mark1:5) · [LUK 1:8](https://ref.ly/Mark1:8)

## ἔχιδνα (id: 002204000000000)

ἔχιδνα
======

ἔχιδνα (G2191)

noun

4\.53 · Animals · Reptiles and Other 'Creeping Things'

asp, cobra, viper kind of poisonous snake; associated with lurking danger The terms ἀσπίς and ἔχιδνα\[a] may be used to refer to almost any kind of poisonous snake, and since at least some type of poisonous snake occurs in practically all parts of the world, there is usually little difficulty involved in finding an appropriate term. One can, however, always employ a descriptive expression, for example, ‘a snake whose bite causes death.’

[MAT 12:34](https://ref.ly/Matt12:34) · [ACT 28:3](https://ref.ly/John28:3)

88\.123 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Bad, Evil, Harmful, Damaging

evil person, viper, snake (a figurative extension of meaning of ἔχιδνα\[a] ‘viper,’ [4\.53](#4.53)) a dangerous and despised person One must exercise due caution in rendering ἔχιδνα in [MAT 12:34](https://ref.ly/Matt12:34) and ὄφις\[b] in [MAT 23:33](https://ref.ly/Matt23:33), since in some cultures snakes are regarded as being particularly wise and clever, and therefore an expression such as ‘brood of vipers’ could be a compliment. In some languages important distinctions are made between different types of snakes, and therefore connotations may vary considerably.

[MAT 3:7](https://ref.ly/Matt3:7) · [MAT 12:34](https://ref.ly/Matt12:34) · [MAT 23:33](https://ref.ly/Matt23:33) · [LUK 3:7](https://ref.ly/Mark3:7)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 12:34; Acts 28:3; Matthew 3:7; Matthew 23:33; Luke 3:7

to have, to contain a marker of designations of content

[ACT 23:25](https://ref.ly/John23:25) · [ACT 27:39](https://ref.ly/John27:39) · [1CO 12:12](https://ref.ly/Rom12:12) · [HEB 9:4](https://ref.ly/Phlm9:4) · [HEB 9:4](https://ref.ly/Phlm9:4)

31\.28 · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Acknowledge

**Collocation:** ἔχω ἐν ἐπιγνώσει

to acknowledge (an idiom, literally: to have in recognition) to recognize something as being what it truly is For another interpretation of ἐπίγνωσις in [ROM 1:28](https://ref.ly/Acts1:28), see [28\.2](#28.2).

[ROM 1:28](https://ref.ly/Acts1:28)

57\.99 · Possess, Transfer, Exchange · Give

**Collocation:** ἔχω κοινός

to share, to share with one another (an idiom, literally: to have in common) to share with one another equitably The mutuality of sharing may be expressed in some languages as ‘each person shared with all of the rest’ or ‘each person gave to the others and received from the others.’

[ACT 2:44](https://ref.ly/John2:44)

90\.83 · Case · Experiencer

**Collocation:** ἔχω μέρος ἐν

to experience together with, to share in experiencing (an idiom, literally: to have a part in) to experience along with others

[REV 20:6](https://ref.ly/Jude20:6)

24\.59 · Sensory Events and States · Hear

**Collocation:** ἔχω οὖς

to be able to hear, can hear (an idiom, literally: to have ear) to be able to hear, with the implication of being expected to hear or having the obligation to hear (with a further implication of related mental activity) In rendering the idiom ἔχω οὖς in [REV 2:7](https://ref.ly/Jude2:7), the implication is that the individuals involved ought to be able to hear and pay attention.

[MAT 11:15](https://ref.ly/Matt11:15) · [MAT 13:9](https://ref.ly/Matt13:9) · [MAT 13:43](https://ref.ly/Matt13:43) · [MRK 4:9](https://ref.ly/Matt4:9) · [MRK 4:23](https://ref.ly/Matt4:23) · [REV 2:7](https://ref.ly/Jude2:7)

33\.285 · Communication · Foretell, Tell Fortunes

**Collocation:** ἔχω πνεῦμα πύθωνα

to be a fortuneteller (an idiom, literally: to have a spirit of python, an appositional construction in Greek) to tell people for pay what would happen to them in the future

[ACT 16:16](https://ref.ly/John16:16)4

23\.151 · Physiological Processes and States · Sickness, Disease, Weakness

**Collocation:** ἐσχάτως ἔχω

to be very sick, to be about to die (an idiom, literally: to be at an extreme) to be very sick, with the implication of imminent death

[MRK 5:23](https://ref.ly/Matt5:23)

23\.148 · Physiological Processes and States · Sickness, Disease, Weakness

**Collocation:** κακῶς ἔχω

to be ill, to be sick (to some extent equivalent to the English idiom to be bad off in speaking of ill health) (an idiom, literally: to have badly, or: to fare badly) to be in a bad state, to be ill

[MAT 4:24](https://ref.ly/Matt4:24) · [MAT 8:16](https://ref.ly/Matt8:16) · [MAT 9:12](https://ref.ly/Matt9:12) · [MAT 14:35](https://ref.ly/Matt14:35) · [MRK 1:32](https://ref.ly/Matt1:32) · [MRK 1:34](https://ref.ly/Matt1:34) · [MRK 2:17](https://ref.ly/Matt2:17) · [MRK 6:55](https://ref.ly/Matt6:55) · [LUK 5:31](https://ref.ly/Mark5:31) · [LUK 7:2](https://ref.ly/Mark7:2)

23\.129 · Physiological Processes and States · Health, Vigor, Strength

**Collocation:** καλῶς ἔχω

to be well, to be healthy (an idiom, literally: to have well) the state of being healthy, well (in contrast with sickness) In a number of languages health is expressed only in terms of strength, for ‘to be well’ is ‘to be strong.’ In other languages, however, to be well or to be healthy is a negation of illness or sickness, so that in [MRK 16:18](https://ref.ly/Matt16:18) one may translate ‘they will place their hands on those who are sick and these people will become not sick.’

[MRK 16:18](https://ref.ly/Matt16:18)

23\.50 · Physiological Processes and States · Birth, Procreation

**Collocation:** κοίτην ἔχω

pregnant, to be pregnant (an idiom, literally: to have bed, though in combination with ἔχω, κοίτη early acquired the metonymic meaning of sexual relations) to be in a state of pregnancy This expression contrasts with the meaning of συλλαμβάνω\[b], [23\.49](#23.49), in that it seems to focus more upon the state of pregnancy rather than the event of conception.

[ROM 9:10](https://ref.ly/Acts9:10)

83\.29 · Spacial Positions · At, Beside, Near, Far

neighboring (a participial form of ἔχω\[a] ‘to have,’ [57\.1](#57.1)) a position contiguous to another position

[MRK 1:38](https://ref.ly/Matt1:38)

23\.50 · Physiological Processes and States · Birth, Procreation

**Collocation:** ἐν γαστρὶ ἔχω

pregnant, to be pregnant (an idiom, literally: to have in the womb) to be in a state of pregnancy This expression contrasts with the meaning of συλλαμβάνω\[b], [23\.49](#23.49), in that it seems to focus more upon the state of pregnancy rather than the event of conception.\|It is impossible to determine whether in [MAT 1:23](https://ref.ly/Matt1:23) (ἐν γαστρὶ ἕξει καὶ τέξεται υἱόν ‘she will be pregnant and bear a son’) the focus is upon the state of pregnancy or the fact of conception (see [23\.49](#23.49)).

[MAT 1:18](https://ref.ly/Matt1:18) · [MAT 1:23](https://ref.ly/Matt1:23) · [MRK 13:17](https://ref.ly/Matt13:17) · [LUK 21:23](https://ref.ly/Mark21:23) · [1TH 5:3](https://ref.ly/Col5:3) · [REV 12:2](https://ref.ly/Jude12:2)

67\.208 · Time · Units of Time with Reference to Other Units or Points of Time: Yesterday, Today, Next Day

**Collocation:** τῇ ἐχομένῃ

the next day, on the next day a day immediately following a previous day5

[LUK 13:33](https://ref.ly/Mark13:33) · [ACT 20:15](https://ref.ly/John20:15) · [ACT 21:26](https://ref.ly/John21:26)

1 Ownership usually entails to a greater or lesser degree the social sanctions of the right by the owner to use the object in question and the obligation by non\-owners to recognize that right and to desist from such usage. 2 This meaning of have/possess contrasts with hold, grasp (Domain 18\), the whole/part relation (Domain 63\), to be in a relationship to (Domain 89\), to be in a state or condition (Domain 13\), and to have authority, power, or control over persons or objects, either sociologically or physically (Domain 37\). 3 MAT 3:4 One might argue that ἔχω in [MAT 3:4](https://ref.ly/Matt3:4) means merely ‘to possess’ ([57\.1](#57.1)), but obviously there is more in the context of [MAT 3:4](https://ref.ly/Matt3:4) than just possessing certain types of clothing. What is relevant is the habitual wearing of such clothing. 4 ACT 16:16 The phrase ἔχω πνεῦμα πύθωνα may focus upon the capacity to be a fortuneteller, but the implication is that the individual involved served as a fortuneteller. It is, however, possible to treat this expression in a somewhat different manner, as indicated in [12\.48](#12.48). 5 Rather than treat these phrases as lexical units, one can interpret them as elliptical expressions with the omission of the dative of ἡμέρα\[a] day ([67\.178](#67.178)).

## ἕως (id: 002206000000000)

ἕως
===

ἕως (G2193)

prep

67\.119 · Time · Duration of Time with Reference to Some Point of Time: Until, Delay, Still, From

until, to, at last, at length the continuous extent of time up to a point1 The phrase εἰ μὴ ὅταν is often translated as ‘until’ (for example, μηδενὶ ἅ εἶδον διηγήσωνταιεἰ μὴ ὅταν ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου ἐκ νεκρῶν ἀναστῇ ‘not to tell anybody what they have seen until the Son of Man has risen from death’ [MRK 9:9](https://ref.ly/Matt9:9)), but this phrase may be analyzed as meaning ‘if it is not when,’ an expression which is semantically equivalent to ‘until.’

67\.139 · Time · Duration of Time with Reference to Some Unit of Time: During, In, While, Throughout

as long as, while an extent of time of the same length as another extent or unit of time

[MRK 6:45](https://ref.ly/Matt6:45) · [MRK 14:32](https://ref.ly/Matt14:32) · [JHN 9:4](https://ref.ly/Luke9:4)

84\.19 · Spacial Extensions · Extension To a Goal

to, up to, as far as extension up to or as far as a goal2

[MAT 2:9](https://ref.ly/Matt2:9) · [MAT 11:23](https://ref.ly/Matt11:23) · [MAT 11:23](https://ref.ly/Matt11:23) · [MAT 24:27](https://ref.ly/Matt24:27) · [MAT 24:31](https://ref.ly/Matt24:31) · [MAT 26:58](https://ref.ly/Matt26:58) · [MAT 27:51](https://ref.ly/Matt27:51) · [MRK 13:27](https://ref.ly/Matt13:27) · [MRK 14:54](https://ref.ly/Matt14:54) · [MRK 15:38](https://ref.ly/Matt15:38) · [LUK 2:15](https://ref.ly/Mark2:15) · [LUK 4:29](https://ref.ly/Mark4:29) · [LUK 4:42](https://ref.ly/Mark4:42) · [LUK 10:15](https://ref.ly/Mark10:15) · [LUK 10:15](https://ref.ly/Mark10:15) · [LUK 23:5](https://ref.ly/Mark23:5) · [LUK 24:50](https://ref.ly/Mark24:50) · [JHN 2:7](https://ref.ly/Luke2:7) · [ACT 1:8](https://ref.ly/John1:8) · [ACT 9:38](https://ref.ly/John9:38) · [ACT 11:19](https://ref.ly/John11:19) · [ACT 11:22](https://ref.ly/John11:22) · [ACT 13:47](https://ref.ly/John13:47) · [ACT 17:14](https://ref.ly/John17:14) · [ACT 17:15](https://ref.ly/John17:15) · [ACT 21:5](https://ref.ly/John21:5) · [ACT 23:23](https://ref.ly/John23:23) · [ACT 26:11](https://ref.ly/John26:11) · [2CO 12:2](https://ref.ly/1Cor12:2) · [HEB 8:11](https://ref.ly/Phlm8:11)

78\.51 · Degree · Up To, As Much As, To the Degree That \[Marked Extent of Degree]

to the point of, to the extent of, to the degree that, up to a degree extending to a particular point as marked by the context In a number of languages it is difficult to express in a succinct manner a degree which reaches to a particular point. The same meaning, however, may often be expressed in a somewhat different form. For example, in [MAT 26:38](https://ref.ly/Matt26:38) one may translate ‘my soul is very sorrowful; it is just as though I were dying.’

[MAT 26:38](https://ref.ly/Matt26:38) · [MRK 14:34](https://ref.ly/Matt14:34) · [LUK 22:51](https://ref.ly/Mark22:51) · [2CO 1:13](https://ref.ly/1Cor1:13)

59\.21 · Quantity · Much, Little \[Masses, Collectives, Extensions]

as much as, up to the extent of a quantity In a number of languages it may be necessary to restructure somewhat the expression ‘up to half of my kingdom,’ for example, ‘I will give you half of my kingdom but not more than half.’

[MAT 18:21](https://ref.ly/Matt18:21) · [MAT 18:22](https://ref.ly/Matt18:22) · [MAT 18:22](https://ref.ly/Matt18:22) · [MRK 6:23](https://ref.ly/Matt6:23) · [ACT 8:10](https://ref.ly/John8:10) · [ROM 3:12](https://ref.ly/Acts3:12)

67\.139 · Time · Duration of Time with Reference to Some Unit of Time: During, In, While, Throughout

**Collocation:** ἕως ὅτου

as long as, while an extent of time of the same length as another extent or unit of time

[MAT 5:25](https://ref.ly/Matt5:25)

67\.139 · Time · Duration of Time with Reference to Some Unit of Time: During, In, While, Throughout

**Collocation:** ἕως οὗ

as long as, while an extent of time of the same length as another extent or unit of time

[MAT 14:22](https://ref.ly/Matt14:22) · [MAT 26:36](https://ref.ly/Matt26:36)

1 ἕως, ἄχρι, and μέχρι frequently occur with a postposed marker of indefinite temporal reference: οὗ, occurring with all three markers, and ὅτου with ἕως. 2 It is probable that ἕως\[c], ἄχρι\[c], and μέχρι\[b] differ slightly in meaning, but this cannot be readily determined from existing contexts.
